- Primary operator: oncall SRE or evaluator (role: trigger and lead investigation).
- Supporting owners: model owner (applied-ml), runtime owner (serving-runtime), infra owner (eng-infra).
- Escalation path: if confidence > 70% of production breakage cause, escalate to Model Rollback channel and Product Impact lead.

Signals and preconditions (what to gather before interactive steps):
1) Alert payload: RSI delta, slice id, example probe ids, and time window.
2) Baseline artifacts: golden prompt set for slice, production model id and commit hash, quant-profile id, last successful probe timestamps.
3) Telemetry extracts: token-latency distribution, KV cache hit ratio, embedding drift score, and trace id for an affected request.

Required telemetry and fast checks (run these first):
- rwd-metrics query examples:
  - rwd-metrics query "RSI(slice=billing-invoice, window=6h)"
  - rwd-metrics query "kv_cache_hit_rate(model=v2, window=1h)"
- Quick thresholds to validate: 
  - RSI drop > 5%: high priority.
  - Median token latency increase > 50ms: possible runtime regression.
  - KV cache hit ratio change > 10%: suspect cache policy/serialization change.

Interactive investigation steps (operator workflow):
1) Isolate representative probes
   - Command: rwd-eval probe-select --slice billing-invoice --severity high --limit 20 --output probes.json
   - Inspect a sample of probes for immediate pattern changes (format, tokenization anomalies, hallucination markers).
2) Re-run probes against shadow model variants
   - Run: rwd-eval debug run --probes probes.json --model rwd-model:v2 --shadow-only --parallel 8 --output run-v2.json
   - Then run against previous model: rwd-eval debug run --probes probes.json --model rwd-model:v1 --shadow-only --output run-v1.json
   - Diff: jq -S '.responses[] | {id: .probe_id, text: .text}' run-v2.json > out-v2.ndjson; jq -S '.responses[] | {id: .probe_id, text: .text}' run-v1.json > out-v1.ndjson; diff out-v1.ndjson out-v2.ndjson
3) Inspect intermediate artifacts
   - Tokenization: rwd-inspect tokenizer --model rwd-model:v2 --input "<example prompt>"
   - KV cache trace (sample): rwd-trace kv --trace-id TRACE-12345 --limit 200
   - Kernel scheduling: rwd-trace kernels --trace-id TRACE-12345
4) Hypothesis generation
   - Use artifacts above to produce 1–3 hypotheses (e.g., quantized attention kernel introduced off-by-one, prompt truncation due to new tokenization).
5) Quick micro-experiments (non-disruptive)
   - Use local simulation flags: rwd-eval debug run --model rwd-model:v2 --probe probes.json --flags "--disable-quant-fallback --force-512-context" --shadow-only
   - If hypothesis relates to quantization, try a hybrid fallback: rwd-deploy canary --model rwd-model:v2 --quant-profile q-safe --target-pct 0.5 --shadow-beta
6) Decision matrix
   - If difference between v1 and v2 for critical probes > severity threshold (default 10% relative failure), recommend one of:
     a) Immediate rollback to v1 (if customer impact is P0 and mitigation urgent).
     b) Canary with reduced quant aggressively disabled and close monitoring.
     c) Patch model prompt wrapper (short-term) + medium-term model retraining.
7) Safe rollback checklist (if chosen)
   - Owner confirmation: model owner acknowledges rollback finger.
   - Runbooks: rwd-deploy rollback --model rwd-model:v1 --force --change-id RB-<ticket> 
   - Post-rollback monitoring: open-monitoring dashboard "rollback-watch" and alert on RSI for 30 minutes.
8) Postmortem and tagging
   - Create a short incident entry with probe ids, hypothesis, experiment artifacts, and final remediation. Tag model artifacts with label: "regression-2026-<shortid>".

Heuristics for blame (quick guide):
- If tokenization diffs align with changes in token-lexicon or new special tokens: blame upstream tokenizer/merge.
- If answers diverge only after 200 tokens and KV cache hit rate drops: blame KV cache eviction policy or cache serialization changes.
- If deterministic seeds produce nondeterministic outputs across runs: suspect scheduling/kernel regression in runtime.
- If differences appear only with quant profiles: suspect quantization thresholds or emergency mixed-precision fallback.

Automated helpers (scripts & services):
- rwd-eval fast-diff: produces slice-level delta report (path: /tools/rwd-eval/fast-diff).
- rwd-debugger-ui: dashboard that mounts probe population and allows side-by-side comparison (internal URL: https://console/internal/debugger).
- scripts/regression-hunt/auto-gather.sh — orchestrates telemetry gathers for an alert id.

SLOs and thresholds (operational):
| Metric | Alert threshold | Operator action | Escalation window |
|-------|-----------------|----------------|------------------|
| RSI (slice) | drop > 5% absolute or > 8% relative | Start interactive debugger | 30 min |
| Median token latency | +50ms | Inspect runtime traces | 60 min |
| KV cache hit rate | -10% | Inspect cache policy | 60 min |

Safe rollout recommendations:
- Prefer shadow-only experiments for hypothesis validation.
- Canary target size should not exceed 1% of production traffic unless safety validated.
- Any canary that changes quant profile must be labeled and limited to < 0.2% traffic until 12h of telemetry passes.

Audit and compliance:
- All debugger commands that mutate production state must emit audit_event with fields: operator, alert_id, action, before_model_id, after_model_id, ticket_id.
- Audit log location: s3://redwood-audit/regression-debugger/YYYY/MM/DD/<ticket>.json

Runbook snippets (examples):
- Start a focused debug session and gather probes:
  rwd-eval session start --alert RSI-4573 --owner priya.natarajan --mode interactive --out session-4573.tar.gz
- Re-run probes against previous artifact:
  rwd-eval debug run --probes session-4573/probes.json --model rwd-model:v1 --shadow-only --parallel 12 --out re-run-v1.json
- Canary deploy with quant fallback disabled:
  rwd-deploy canary --model rwd-model:v2 --quant-profile q-safe --target-pct 0.5 --reason "regression-debug" --ticket RG-4573

Post-incident tasks and knowledge capture:
1) Populate the regression index card (template path: /templates/regression-index-card).
2) Add annotated probe examples to the canonical prompt set for the slice.
3) If change was code/infra, open an ADR referencing the root cause and mitigation plan.

Appendix: Useful debugging commands and endpoints
- Telemetry queries: rwd-metrics query "metric(args)"
- Trace viewer: https://console/internal/traces?trace_id=TRACE-<id>
- Debug UI: https://console/internal/debugger
- Audit viewer: https://console/internal/audit?source=regression-debugger

Related pages
- /applied-ml-and-evals/regression-triage/probe-farm-orchestration-for-rare-regressions-2026
- /applied-ml-and-evals/eval-harness/shadow-to-prod-eval-continuity-guidelines-2026

Contact and escalation
- Oncall SRE: #oncall-sre (Slack)
- Model owners: team-applied-ml@redwood.com
- Emergency Pager: PagerDuty — Redwood ML Critical

This playbook is reviewed quarterly or after any P0 regression event.

title: Adaptive Eval Funnel and Cost-Aware Sampling Playbook
preview:
Overview:

This playbook defines the Adaptive Eval Funnel (AEF) — a standardized, cost-aware sampling and prioritization pipeline for running continuous evaluation at scale. AEF is designed to reduce token and compute spend by 40–70% relative to naive full-scan evals while preserving coverage for high-risk slices and regression detection sensitivity.

Goals:
- Maintain high-fidelity regression detection on high-risk slices (precision >= 0.90 on targeted failure classes).
- Reduce end-to-end eval cost per release by 40% (measured in GPU-minutes and token units).
- Provide reproducible, auditable sampling decisions and a transparent budget allocation for product/tenant workloads.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: applies to model onboarding, nightly and pre-release eval runs, and production regression triage workload mirroring. Does NOT replace small-sample human evaluation for subjective quality checks.
- Audience: applied-ML engineers, eval platform owners, product reliability engineers, and model steward teams.

Definitions:
- Funnel stage: a logical step in the AEF (seed, stratified-scan, focused-verify, heavy-sim).
- Slice: a partition of workload (intent category, customer tier, language, prompt-length bin, etc.).
- Budget unit: our internal token+GPU-minute currency; 1 unit = 10k input tokens + 1 GPU-minute at p3 equivalent.

When to use AEF:
1) Pre-release validation for model promotions.
2) Nightly smoke and slice-focused monitoring for production models.
3) Regression triage when a large surface area prevents exhaustive replay.

High-level funnel (four stages):
1. Seed discovery (low cost)
   - Objective: locate candidate failing slices cheaply.
   - Inputs: lightweight classifiers, anomaly scoring over telemetry, recent customer Qs, error logs.
   - Typical compute: <5% of total budget.
2. Stratified scan (medium cost)
   - Objective: broad coverage with stratified sampling across prioritized dimensions.
   - Method: stratify by slice weights; draw proportional samples with minimum cell size.
   - Typical compute: 25–35% of budget.
3. Focused verify (targeted)
   - Objective: validate potential regressions with larger, deterministic sample sets per flagged slice.
   - Method: fixed-seed replays, ensemble-checks, human-in-the-loop for ambiguous cases.
   - Typical compute: 30–40% of budget.
4. Heavy simulation (expensive)
   - Objective: full-scan or synthetic amplification for highest-risk or regulatory slices.
   - Triggered only if FDR/precision thresholds not met or downstream business metrics at risk.
   - Typical compute: 10–30% of budget (on demand).

Sampling configuration (recommended defaults):
| Parameter | Default | Rationale |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| monthly_budget_units | 500 | Enough to run nightly stratified scans + 2 pre-release runs for medium-sized products |
| min_cell_size | 50 | Minimum per-slice sample to estimate proportions with modest variance |
| seed_detector_threshold | 0.7 | Anomaly score threshold to escalate from seed to stratified-scan |
| focused_verify_size | 300 | Per-flagged-slice sample size for deterministic checks |
| heavy_sim_trigger_precision | 0.85 | If focused verify precision < this, run heavy sim |

Priority weighting scheme (example):
1) Safety / content policy risk: weight x3
2) High-revenue customers (top 10% ARR): weight x2
3) New prompt patterns / long context: weight x1.5
4) Low-frequency slices: floor sample for minimum visibility

Operational procedure (step-by-step):
1) Configure budget and slice definitions in eval-harness config (file: eval-configs/aef.yml). Make sure labels map to telemetry fields and tenant IDs.
2) Run Seed discovery job (weekly/nightly). Command: 
```bash
redwood-eval run --pipeline aef --stage seed --config eval-configs/aef.yml --output s3://eval-outputs/aef/seed/$(date +%F)
```
3) Seed job produces ranked slice candidates. Automatically promote top N where anomaly_score >= seed_detector_threshold to Stratified scan. Document promotions in the run artifact.
4) Run Stratified scan with budget clamps. Use stratified-sampling operator with deterministic RNG seed for reproducibility. CLI: 
```bash
redwood-eval run --pipeline aef --stage stratified --config eval-configs/aef.yml --budget-units 150 --seed 20260301
```
5) Post-process stratified results to compute per-slice metrics (accuracy delta, entropy change, top-k hallucination rate). If any slice metric breaches alert thresholds (see "Monitoring and thresholds"), schedule Focused verify.
6) Focused verify is launched with deterministic seeds and ensemble baselines. Results produce a triage report with suggested root-cause tags (prompt-sensitivity, quantization-delta, kv-cache-regression, model-parameter-shift). Human reviewer assigned automatically for ambiguous failures.
7) Heavy simulation is run only with explicit owner approval; include cost estimate and rollback action in the run ticket.

Monitoring and thresholds:
- Metrics collected per slice: accuracy_delta, top3_confidence_drop, hallucination_rate, token_cost_per_input, p95_latency.
- Alert thresholds (examples):
  - accuracy_delta <= -0.06 -> auto-priority alert
  - hallucination_rate increase >= 0.03 absolute -> safety review
  - token_cost_per_input increase >= 20% -> cost-investigation
  - p95_latency increase >= 200ms -> perf triage

Acceptance and escalation rules:
- If a high-weight slice (safety or top-revenue) breaches accuracy_delta <= -0.04 in Focused verify, block promotion and open a model regression incident with incident severity S2.
- If multiple mid-weight slices show correlated failures across 3+ prompts, escalate to Applied ML on-call for bisecting.

Reproducibility and auditability:
- All runs must store: config.yaml, RNG seed, model sha, quant-profile id, inference kernel version, and the full input sample set.
- Use artifact naming convention: aef/{model_sha}/{run-date}/{stage}/{budget-id}.zip

Cost control knobs and tradeoffs:
- min_cell_size: raising reduces false positives but increases cost.
- stratified_coverage_ratio: 0.2–0.6 recommended. Lower values favor targeted checks.
- use_kv_cache_replay: toggle; caches reduce token compute for long context but may obscure prompt-sensitivity failures.

Example evaluation config (eval-configs/aef.yml):
```yaml
pipeline: aef
budget_units: 300
priority_weights:
  safety: 3
  revenue: 2
  long_context: 1.5
slices:
  - name: billing-intent
    selectors: ["intent:billing", "lang:en"]
  - name: long-context-technical
    selectors: ["prompt_len:>1024", "topic:technical"]
```

Triage playbook (on detection of regression):
1) Confirm reproducibility with Focused verify using stored RNG seed.
2) Run bisect suite across model versions and quant-profiles.
3) If quantization implicated, tag with quant-profile id and set rollback to float-weighted baseline.
4) If prompt cluster implicated, add representative prompts to the regression prompt-set and schedule patch evaluation.
5) Publish incident summary to the model pager channel and attach artifact bundle.

Ownership and SLAs:
- Eval platform owner: Applied ML platform team (oncall rotation: applied-ml-platform@redwood.com) — SLA: respond within 2 business hours for pipeline failures.
- Model steward (per model): required to respond to S2 regression incidents within 4 business hours.

Metrics to report weekly:
- total_budget_units_consumed
- number_of_slices_flagged
- avg_time_to_triage (hours)
- precision_of_seed_to_flag pipeline (measured vs focused verify)

Appendix: investigative tools and helpers
- Commands:
  - List past runs: redwood-eval list --pipeline aef --model <sha>
  - Fetch artifacts: redwood-eval fetch --run-id <id> --dest ./artifacts
- JSON example of a slice flag record:
```json
{"slice_name":"billing-intent","anomaly_score":0.82,"flag_reason":"accuracy_delta:-0.07","run_id":"aef-20260301-strat-42"}
```

Related policies and pages:
- See prompt-coverage matrix and drift repair guide for how to define slices and minimum coverage targets.
- See regression triage canonical runbook for incident escalation templates.

Revision history:
- 2026-02-18: initial published by Ava Chen.
- 2026-03-01: clarified heavy-simulation gating and added cost knobs by request of reviewers.

Contact:
- Playbook owner: Ava Chen (ava.chen@redwood.ai)
- Platform on-call: applied-ml-platform@redwood.com

title: Latency and Token-Budgeted Prompt Design Guide
preview:
Summary:
This guide defines a repeatable design and validation workflow for authoring prompts when you have hard latency or token-budget constraints. It covers principled prompt composition, token accounting, cache-priming patterns, integration tests for the eval harness, and rollout checks for model promotion. The target reader is engineers and applied-ML owners preparing prompts for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).

Scope and objectives:
- Scope: customer-facing text- and chat-style prompts used in production inference paths (single-turn and multi-turn). Does not cover multimodal binary payload formats.
- Objectives:
  - Ensure prompts meet latency SLOs and token budgets for cost predictability.
  - Maximize reuse of KV/prefix caches and enable safe quantized deployments.
  - Provide testable templates and an automated validation checklist for onboarding.

Key definitions:
- Token budget: maximum average tokens per request (input + expected output) allocated to a route.
- Latency SLO: 95th percentile latency target for generation portion of a request (example: 500 ms for 128-token responses on p4d).
- Priming slice: compact context intended to be cached (prefix) and not sent every request.

Principles for budgeted prompt design:
1) Make context scannable and compressible:
   - Prefer concise, semantically dense instructions (one short sentence per instruction).
   - Replace long enumerations with named anchors and include a short lookup when needed.
2) Partition mutable vs stable context:
   - Place stable facts, policy, or persona in a priming slice (cacheable).
   - Place request-specific data in the transient context (per-request tokens).
3) Budget first, optimize second:
   - Choose a hard token budget per route (example budgets below).
   - If quality drops, prefer targeted prompt edits and reranking over increasing budget.
4) Design for cache hits and graceful degradation:
   - Structure prompts so missing cache entries are safe: fallbacks should still be valid albeit more verbose.
5) Test under quantized kernels and batching:
   - Validate prompts with the quantization profile(s) that will be used in production.

Token budget examples (suggested starting points):
| Route class | Input budget (tokens) | Output budget (tokens) | Notes |
|-------------|----------------------:|-----------------------:|-------|
| Low-latency short response | 64 | 64 | Mobile search snippets, chat suggestions |
| Medium-latency assistant | 256 | 256 | Feature assistants with context and follow-ups |
| High-throughput embedding-driven | 128 | 64 | Embedding-based rerank + short answer |

Prompt composition templates (text):
- Compact instruction template (cacheable prefix):
  "SYSTEM: You are an assistant that answers concisely. STYLE: bullet-brief.
   FACTS: [stable facts — place in priming slice].
   --END PREFIX--
   USER: <customer prompt or example>"

- Split-prompt example for KV caching:
  1) Priming (cached): "Docs summary: <3-line abstract>. Safety: refuse hallucinations. Style: concise."
  2) Ephemeral (per-request): "User query: {query}. Context snippets: {topK passages}. Task: answer in <= 64 tokens."

Token accounting rules (practical):
- Count tokens using the production tokenizer for the model family.
- Include: system + user + instruction + expected output budget. Exclude: wire/transport framing added by SDKs (we assert those are negligible but verify).
- Formula (string to implement in CI):
  expected_total = ceil(tokenize(prefix)) + ceil(max_input_tokens) + ceil(max_output_tokens) + safety_margin
  where safety_margin = max(16, ceil(0.05 * expected_total))

Service-level SLO guidance (example defaults):
- p95 generation latency: 500 ms for <= 128 output tokens on Dedicated p4d (single-request, no batching).
- Token budget compliance: <1% of requests exceed the configured budget in steady-state testing.
- Cache hit contribution: primed routes should target >= 60% cache-hit rate in steady traffic to justify the split.

Validation checklist (pre-promotion):
1) Tokenize all templates with production tokenizer and confirm expected_total <= route_budget for 99th pct of test inputs.
2) Run the sample prompt family through the quantized model artifacts (if using quant). Compare response-quality metrics (ROUGE/EM or task-specific thresholds) against baseline model.
3) Shadow-run the route for at least 24h (or 1M requests equivalent) with eval harness:
   - Verify p95 latency meets SLO under target batching levels.
   - Verify token-budget exceedance rate <1%.
   - Collect cache-hit telemetry.
4) Execute functional tests: safety-sentinels, hallucination probes, and continuity checks for multi-turn sequences.
5) Record rollout plan and rollback criteria in the release ticket.

Automated eval harness integration (example YAML):
- name: prompt-priming-budget-check
  type: shadow-scan
  routes: ["assistant-short", "assistant-medium"]
  samples: sampler.lookup("production-probe-anchored")
  checks:
    - name: token-budget-violation
      assert: percentile(tokens_total, 99) <= ${route.token_budget}
    - name: latency-p95
      assert: p95_generation_latency_ms <= ${route.p95_slo_ms}
    - name: quality-delta
      metric: fidelity_score
      assert: delta <= 0.03

Design patterns and anti-patterns:
- Pattern: "Prefix compression" — move stable long lists into a single-line compressed annotation with an index reference. This yields better cache behavior and smaller prefixes.
- Pattern: "Two-stage answering" — generate a compact plan in the first micro-call (<= 32 tokens) and expand in an allowed secondary call when more tokens are required; use only when latency SLOs permit.
- Anti-pattern: embedding a long dump of unrelated documentation in the per-request context — kills cacheability and unpredictably increases cost.

Rolling out with quantized/models mix:
- If deploying a quantized artifact, add a quant-fallback policy:
  - Route config should declare compatible variants: [fp16, int8-alpha].
  - On increased error budget or unexpected fidelity regressions, traffic must automatically revert to the last-known-good variant.
- Example rollout policy: gradual: 0% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 4 days with canary quality gates at each step.

Monitoring and telemetry (required):
- Per-route: p50/p95/p99 generation latency, tokens_in, tokens_out, token_budget_exceed_rate, cache_hit_rate, fidelity_delta.
- Alerts: token_budget_exceed_rate > 1% for 30m; p95 latency breach > 20% of SLO for 15m; fidelity_delta > configured threshold.

On-call playbook (short):
1) Triage alert: check route telemetry dashboard and recent eval harness run.
2) If token budget exceedances: roll traffic to smaller output_budget or enable autoscaling; if immediate fix required, rollback to previous prompt template tag.
3) If fidelity regressions observed under quant: trigger auto-revert to fp16 variant and open a regression triage ticket.

Ownership and lifecycle:
- Prompt owners: the product/feature team that owns the surface area. They are responsible for template changes and providing sample probes.
- Applied ML: responsible for quantitative validation (eval harness runs, quant checks).
- Runtime team: ensures metrics, SLOs, and cache telemetry are available for the route.

Appendix A — Example short prompt family: 
- Priming slice (cached):
  "Assistant role: concise technical helper. Allowed formats: bullet list, short paragraph. Refuse to invent facts. 
   Policy: prefer public documentation only."
- Dynamic slice (per request):
  "Question: {user_query}
   TopK: {top3_docs}
   Task: answer in <= 64 tokens, include one-line citation."

Appendix B — Example token-accounting pseudocode (run in CI):
```
# pseudo
def token_budget_check(prefix, sample_inputs, max_output):
    tokenizer = load_prod_tokenizer()
    prefix_tokens = tokenizer.count(prefix)
    for s in sample_inputs:
        total = prefix_tokens + tokenizer.count(s) + max_output
        margin = max(16, int(0.05 * total))
        assert total + margin <= ROUTE_BUDGET
```

References and links:
- See eval harness runbook for shadow scans and orchestration.
- Quantization profiling playbooks for per-workload checks.

Change log:
- 2026-02-10: created (initial draft).
- 2026-03-01: updated token accounting rules and added YAML integration example.

Contact: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai for questions or template merge requests.
text:
Summary:
This guide defines a repeatable design and validation workflow for authoring prompts when you have hard latency or token-budget constraints. It covers principled prompt composition, token accounting, cache-priming patterns, integration tests for the eval harness, and rollout checks for model promotion. The target reader is engineers and applied-ML owners preparing prompts for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).

Scope and objectives:
- Scope: customer-facing text- and chat-style prompts used in production inference paths (single-turn and multi-turn). Does not cover multimodal binary payload formats.
- Objectives:
  - Ensure prompts meet latency SLOs and token budgets for cost predictability.
  - Maximize reuse of KV/prefix caches and enable safe quantized deployments.
  - Provide testable templates and an automated validation checklist for onboarding.

Key definitions:
- Token budget: maximum average tokens per request (input + expected output) allocated to a route.
- Latency SLO: 95th percentile latency target for generation portion of a request (example: 500 ms for 128-token responses on p4d).
- Priming slice: compact context intended to be cached (prefix) and not sent every request.

Principles for budgeted prompt design:
1) Make context scannable and compressible:
   - Prefer concise, semantically dense instructions (one short sentence per instruction).
   - Replace long enumerations with named anchors and include a short lookup when needed.
2) Partition mutable vs stable context:
   - Place stable facts, policy, or persona in a priming slice (cacheable).
   - Place request-specific data in the transient context (per-request tokens).
3) Budget first, optimize second:
   - Choose a hard token budget per route (example budgets below).
   - If quality drops, prefer targeted prompt edits and reranking over increasing budget.
4) Design for cache hits and graceful degradation:
   - Structure prompts so missing cache entries are safe: fallbacks should still be valid albeit more verbose.
5) Test under quantized kernels and batching:
   - Validate prompts with the quantization profile(s) that will be used in production.

Token budget examples (suggested starting points):
| Route class | Input budget (tokens) | Output budget (tokens) | Notes |
|-------------|----------------------:|-----------------------:|-------|
| Low-latency short response | 64 | 64 | Mobile search snippets, chat suggestions |
| Medium-latency assistant | 256 | 256 | Feature assistants with context and follow-ups |
| High-throughput embedding-driven | 128 | 64 | Embedding-based rerank + short answer |

Prompt composition templates (text):
- Compact instruction template (cacheable prefix):
  "SYSTEM: You are an assistant that answers concisely. STYLE: bullet-brief.
   FACTS: [stable facts — place in priming slice].
   --END PREFIX--
   USER: <customer prompt or example>"

- Split-prompt example for KV caching:
  1) Priming (cached): "Docs summary: <3-line abstract>. Safety: refuse hallucinations. Style: concise."
  2) Ephemeral (per-request): "User query: {query}. Context snippets: {topK passages}. Task: answer in <= 64 tokens."

Token accounting rules (practical):
- Count tokens using the production tokenizer for the model family.
- Include: system + user + instruction + expected output budget. Exclude: wire/transport framing added by SDKs (we assert those are negligible but verify).
- Formula (string to implement in CI):
  expected_total = ceil(tokenize(prefix)) + ceil(max_input_tokens) + ceil(max_output_tokens) + safety_margin
  where safety_margin = max(16, ceil(0.05 * expected_total))

Service-level SLO guidance (example defaults):
- p95 generation latency: 500 ms for <= 128 output tokens on Dedicated p4d (single-request, no batching).
- Token budget compliance: <1% of requests exceed the configured budget in steady-state testing.
- Cache hit contribution: primed routes should target >= 60% cache-hit rate in steady traffic to justify the split.

Validation checklist (pre-promotion):
1) Tokenize all templates with production tokenizer and confirm expected_total <= route_budget for 99th pct of test inputs.
2) Run the sample prompt family through the quantized model artifacts (if using quant). Compare response-quality metrics (ROUGE/EM or task-specific thresholds) against baseline model.
3) Shadow-run the route for at least 24h (or 1M requests equivalent) with eval harness:
   - Verify p95 latency meets SLO under target batching levels.
   - Verify token-budget exceedance rate <1%.
   - Collect cache-hit telemetry.
4) Execute functional tests: safety-sentinels, hallucination probes, and continuity checks for multi-turn sequences.
5) Record rollout plan and rollback criteria in the release ticket.

Automated eval harness integration (example YAML):
- name: prompt-priming-budget-check
  type: shadow-scan
  routes: ["assistant-short", "assistant-medium"]
  samples: sampler.lookup("production-probe-anchored")
  checks:
    - name: token-budget-violation
      assert: percentile(tokens_total, 99) <= ${route.token_budget}
    - name: latency-p95
      assert: p95_generation_latency_ms <= ${route.p95_slo_ms}
    - name: quality-delta
      metric: fidelity_score
      assert: delta <= 0.03

Design patterns and anti-patterns:
- Pattern: "Prefix compression" — move stable long lists into a single-line compressed annotation with an index reference. This yields better cache behavior and smaller prefixes.
- Pattern: "Two-stage answering" — generate a compact plan in the first micro-call (<= 32 tokens) and expand in an allowed secondary call when more tokens are required; use only when latency SLOs permit.
- Anti-pattern: embedding a long dump of unrelated documentation in the per-request context — kills cacheability and unpredictably increases cost.

Rolling out with quantized/models mix:
- If deploying a quantized artifact, add a quant-fallback policy:
  - Route config should declare compatible variants: [fp16, int8-alpha].
  - On increased error budget or unexpected fidelity regressions, traffic must automatically revert to the last-known-good variant.
- Example rollout policy: gradual: 0% -> 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 4 days with canary quality gates at each step.

Monitoring and telemetry (required):
- Per-route: p50/p95/p99 generation latency, tokens_in, tokens_out, token_budget_exceed_rate, cache_hit_rate, fidelity_delta.
- Alerts: token_budget_exceed_rate > 1% for 30m; p95 latency breach > 20% of SLO for 15m; fidelity_delta > configured threshold.

On-call playbook (short):
1) Triage alert: check route telemetry dashboard and recent eval harness run.
2) If token budget exceedances: roll traffic to smaller output_budget or enable autoscaling; if immediate fix required, rollback to previous prompt template tag.
3) If fidelity regressions observed under quant: trigger auto-revert to fp16 variant and open a regression triage ticket.

Ownership and lifecycle:
- Prompt owners: the product/feature team that owns the surface area. They are responsible for template changes and providing sample probes.
- Applied ML: responsible for quantitative validation (eval harness runs, quant checks).
- Runtime team: ensures metrics, SLOs, and cache telemetry are available for the route.

Appendix A — Example short prompt family: 
- Priming slice (cached):
  "Assistant role: concise technical helper. Allowed formats: bullet list, short paragraph. Refuse to invent facts. 
   Policy: prefer public documentation only."
- Dynamic slice (per request):
  "Question: {user_query}
   TopK: {top3_docs}
   Task: answer in <= 64 tokens, include one-line citation."

Appendix B — Example token-accounting pseudocode (run in CI):
```
# pseudo
def token_budget_check(prefix, sample_inputs, max_output):
    tokenizer = load_prod_tokenizer()
    prefix_tokens = tokenizer.count(prefix)
    for s in sample_inputs:
        total = prefix_tokens + tokenizer.count(s) + max_output
        margin = max(16, int(0.05 * total))
        assert total + margin <= ROUTE_BUDGET
```

References and links:
- See eval harness runbook for shadow scans and orchestration.
- Quantization profiling playbooks for per-workload checks.

Change log:
- 2026-02-10: created (initial draft).
- 2026-03-01: updated token accounting rules and added YAML integration example.

Contact: priya.natarajan@redwood.ai for questions or template merge requests.

title: Promotion Readiness: Cross-Stage Synthetic Simulation & Quantization Safety Playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook defines a combined evaluation and safety protocol used to decide when a model artifact can be promoted from evaluation (canary/validation) into production routing. It coordinates three corridors of checks: 1) synthetic user simulation to validate behavioral and distributional stability, 2) quantization & kernel-sensitivity safety checks for performance-preserving transforms, and 3) lightweight runtime smoke and canary procedures to exercise operational invariants. The goal is deterministic, reproducible promotion decisions tied to explicit pass/fail signals and rollback criteria.

Scope and Use Cases

- Applies to any model promotion that will be served by Redwood runtime variants where quantized or variant kernels (FP16/INT8/QLoRA-style) are in the promotion path.
- Intended for models with expected production throughput above 1 qps/customer or with regulatory/data-sensitivity constraints (customer PII handling).
- Not required for experimental sandbox promotions under 24h unless flagged by product as critical.

Definitions

- Promotion: the act of moving a model from staging/canary to a production route where customer traffic is served.
- Synthetic Actor: a deterministic user-simulation profile used to exercise prompts and contexts representative of production usage.
- Quant Safety Check: a set of deterministic tests that validate output fidelity and KV-cache integrity across quantized kernels.

Ownership and Roles

- Model Owner (primary): ML engineer who produced the model artifact and is responsible for test bundles, prompt sets, and baseline metrics.
- Eval Owner: engineering squad responsible for running the synthetic actor harness and interpreting results.
- Runtime Owner: infra/runtime team responsible for quantization tooling, kernel selection, and performance baselining.
- Release Approver: product or platform PM with final sign-off for non-emergency promotions.

Acceptance Criteria (summary table)

- For each criterion below the format is: Criterion | Pass Threshold | Primary Signal | Escalation
  - Behavioral stability | Delta <= 1.5% on core pass-rate metrics across prompt-slices | aggregate pass-rate time series (7k sample window) | Auto-fail -> regression-triage
  - Semantic drift | Embedding cosine shift < 0.06 median across intent clusters | embedding distribution KL (staging vs baseline) | Review required
  - Exact-match integrity for CI prompts | 100% for deterministic QA prompts | golden-answer diff report | Auto-fail
  - Quantized fidelity | Top-1 response similarity >= 95% (ROUGE-L or BLEU for fixed tasks), and human-annotated quality delta <= 0.5 on sampled prompts | batch-diff + human spot-check | Rollback & quant-retry
  - KV-cache parity | no cache corruption observed in 10k simulated long-context requests | kernel log parity + checksum | Runtime rollback
  - Latency and tail | p99 < target_latency and p99_tail_spikes < 10% over baseline | telemetry time-series | Canary throttling

Design: Synthetic Actor Harness

- Purpose: emulate real user behavior without shipping customer data. Synthetic actors combine prompt skeletons, variable slot content generators, and timing distributions.
- Actors are stored as JSON bundles: {actor_id, intent_weight, prompt_templates[], context_length_dist, slot_generators[]}. Example actor fragment below.

Example actor JSON (representative):

{
  "actor_id": "support_search_v2",
  "intent_weight": "0.18",
  "prompt_templates": [
    "User: {{query}}
Assistant:",
    "Given the following product description: {{desc}}
Summarize:"
  ],
  "context_length_dist": "poisson(λ=64)",
  "slot_generators": ["wiki-snippet-sampler", "customer-nounizer-v2"]
}

- Scenario catalog (must cover):
  1) Short-form Q&A (<=64 tokens) - 40% weight.
  2) Long-context summarization (>=1024 tokens) - 25% weight.
  3) Multi-step instruction with tool-calls emulation - 10% weight.
  4) Edge adversarial variants (typos, hallucination probes) - 15% weight.
  5) Rare-entity and numeric reasoning - 10% weight.

- Execution: run 3 independent seeds per actor with deterministic RNG to allow diffs. Collect full traces including prompt, logits (sample), KV-cache checksums, and returned tokens. Store artifacts in the eval artifact bucket with the tag: promotion/<model>:<sha>.

Quantization & Kernel Safety Checks

- Purpose: verify that quant transforms and kernel selections do not induce unacceptable semantic/functional regressions.
- Automated checks:
  - Layer-wise output delta: run a 500-sample micro-batch and compute per-layer L2 and cosine delta vs baseline FP32. Thresholds: median L2 < 1e-3, cosine > 0.999.
  - End-to-end behavioral delta: use the synthetic actor prompt set, compute pass-rate deltas and embedding shifts.
  - KV-cache replay: for long-context runs, serialize and replay KV-cache through quantized kernels and validate checksums.
  - Failure-mode detector: fuzz a subset of kernels with perturbed inputs to detect NaNs/inf or divergence outliers.

- Tooling commands (example):
  - Quant bake: 
    redwood-quant bake --model model.tgz --profile int8-s90 --output quantized/model-int8.tgz
  - Layer compare: 
    redwood-compare-layers --base model_fp32 --candidate model_int8 --samples 500 --out report.html

Runtime Smoke Test Suite

- Purpose: exercise operational invariants (metrics, logs, tracing) before opening production traffic.
- Keys:
  - Load profile: synthetic actors at 1x, 5x expected qps for 10 minutes each.
  - Observability checks: ensure traces propagate, token-level billing counters accurate, request IDs preserved.
  - Telemetry asserts: no more than 0.2% errors, p95 latency regression < 10% of baseline.

Canary Promotion Procedure (numbered)

1) Artifact upload: Model owner uploads artifact, test bundles, and promotion metadata to the staging registry.
2) Preflight: CI runs unit tests, deterministic QA prompts, and the synthetic actor harness end-to-end. Reject on auto-fail signals.
3) Quant safety pass: Runtime Owner runs quant checks and signs off or files quant-issue ticket.
4) Smoke run: Deploy to isolated canary pool (1% traffic) with synthetic traffic overlay and 30m observation window.
5) Rollout phases: 1% -> 10% -> 50% -> 100% by automated policy, each stage gated by acceptance criteria evaluation window (30m for small models, 60m for L>8k contexts).
6) Monitoring guardrails: automated rollback triggers include: behavioral stability fail, KV-cache parity fail, p99 latency > threshold, or error rate spike > configured delta.
7) Post-promotion audit: collect artifacts, diff reports, and human spot-check on 200 sampled prompts.

Automated Alerting and Rollback Rules

- Alert channels: PagerDuty to Runtime on severity=1 (KV-cache corruption), Slack #runtime-ops for severity=2, email to model-owner for degraded quality signals.
- Auto-rollback thresholds (examples):
  - Behavioral pass-rate drop >= 2% absolute in sliding 10m window -> automatic rollback.
  - KV-cache checksum mismatch (any) during canary -> immediate rollback and incident start.
  - p99 latency > baseline_p99 * 1.5 for 10 minutes -> gradual throttle and rollback if persists.

Data and Artifact Retention

- All synthetic-run artifacts and diff reports for a promotion must be retained for 90 days under eval-artifacts/<model>/<sha> with TTL tags.
- Human annotation records from the spot-check should be stored with reviewer id and comments.

Failure & Triage Process

- If automated checks fail, create a regression ticket with: title, failing signals, links to artifacts, investigator assignment. Use label: regressions/promotion.
- Regression triage steps:
  1) Reproduce in isolated environment (runtime owner + model owner).
  2) Run layer-diff and kernel-fuzz tests.
  3) If quant-related, attempt quant-profile fallback (e.g., int8->int8-s95 or int16) and re-run harness.
  4) If behavioral, escalate to applied-ml for annotation and root-cause.

Auditability & Sign-off

- Each successful promotion must include the following artifacts in the promotion ticket: artifact SHA, synthetic-run summary report, quant-compare report, smoke-run telemetry snapshot, human spot-check results, release approver signature.
- The Release Approver records the promotion decision and any exceptions in the promotion ticket.

Appendix: Example Eval Command Sequence

1) Bake quant candidate
   redwood-quant bake --model s3://models/foo/sha.tgz --profile int8-s90 --output /tmp/foo-int8.tgz
2) Run harness
   redwood-eval run --actors actors/catalog.json --model /tmp/foo-int8.tgz --out artifacts/foo/sha --seeds 3
3) Layer compare
   redwood-compare-layers --base s3://models/foo/fp32 --candidate /tmp/foo-int8.tgz --samples 500 --out artifacts/foo/layer-report.html

Appendix: Minimum Required Test Bundle (table-like list)

- Deterministic QA prompts: 1k prompts (must include golden answers).
- Actor prompt set: 10k produced prompts across actor catalog.
- Long-context replay corpus: 2k sequences >= 3k tokens each.
- Kernel fuzz harness: 5000 micro-batches designed to expose NaNs/inf.
- Human spot-check: 200 prompts sampled from failing/edge slices.

References and Links

- See: ../model-onboarding/inference-readiness-eval-matrix-and-promotion-playbook-2026.json for baseline promotion policies.
- See: ../quantization-profiles/kv-cache-quant-stability-audit-playbook-2025.json for detailed KV-cache checks.

Notes and Rationale

- Combining synthetic actors with deterministic quant checks reduces dependence on scarce human annotation while still surfacing high-risk regressions. The thresholds above were chosen based on historical regressions observed in Q1–Q3 2025 where early quantized promotions without KV checks produced silent corruption in long-context flows.

Contact

- Primary: Ava Chen (Model Owner liaison) — ava.chen@redwood.ai
- Runtime on-call: see #runtime-oncall rota

Change log

- 2026-02-10: Initial draft.
- 2026-02-17: Added quant-fuzz details and example commands (reviewer: Priya Kapoor).
- 2026-03-01: Updated acceptance thresholds and canary windows after cross-team review (reviewers: Diego Martinez, Liam O'Neill).

title: Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI) and Annotator Adaptation Manual
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI), describes a standardized annotator adaptation protocol, and provides operational runbooks for integrating QSPI into our promotion/evaluation pipeline for quantized artifacts. The goal is to detect, quantify, and remediate semantic or task-specific signal loss introduced by quantization changes while minimizing annotation overhead and preserving production fidelity.

Why this matters:
- Quantization reduces cost but can change subtle tokenization/representation behavior that impacts downstream labelers, rerankers, and function-call correctness.
- Existing throughput-driven checks (latency/peak tokens) miss graded signal degradation affecting annotator agreement and quality SLAs.
- QSPI gives a single, explainable index and a fast path to adapt annotators and probe sets where signal loss is concentrated.

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to model artifacts and runtime variants intended for production promotion (hosted, dedicated, private).
- Intended for mixed-precision and integer quant (e.g., FP16->INT8, 4-bit transforms) and algorithmic kernel changes.
- Not a replacement for full fidelity human-in-the-loop evals — a prioritization and gating metric that maps to annotator effort budgets.

Contents of this manual:
1) Definition and computation of QSPI
2) Probe selection and weighting rules for QSPI
3) Annotator adaptation protocol (AA-1) and fast re-training checklist
4) Thresholds, SLOs, and gating rules for promotion
5) Triage and rollback playbook with CLI examples
6) Observability and dashboarding recommendations
7) Ownership and decision matrix

1) Quantization Signal Preservation Index (QSPI)
Definition: QSPI is a weighted score in [0,100] where 100 == no detectable behavioral difference between baseline (float or golden artifact) and candidate (quantized) across a prioritized probe set. Lower scores indicate larger expected annotation overhead or user-impact risk.
Computation (high-level):
- For each probe i, compute a per-probe similarity s_i in [0,1]. Similarity uses task-aware metrics: exact-match for structured outputs, BLEU/ROUGE/EM for textual templates, embedding cosine + classifier-concordance for rerankers, and functioncall-schema-match for tool-calling.
- Apply a probe weight w_i reflecting traffic, business-criticality, and sensitivity. Weights sum to 1.
- QSPI = 100 * (sum_i w_i * s_i)^alpha where alpha is a conservatism factor (default 1.0).
Example: 50 probes with concentrated weight on top-10 business-critical slices.

2) Probe selection and weighting rules
- Probe types: production-mirror probes (live-anchored), synthetic edgecases (seeded), and canonical unit probes (deterministic). Use the following mix by default: 60% production-mirror, 30% synthetic edgecases, 10% canonical unit probes.
- Weighting heuristic:
  - w_i = base_weight(slice_traffic_rank) * impact_multiplier
  - impact_multiplier = 2.0 for financial/legal slices, 1.5 for functioncall-heavy, 0.8 for low-risk conversational slices.
- Minimum probe coverage: ensure each critical slice has at least 12 probes across seeds/primings to stabilize s_i.

3) Annotator Adaptation Protocol (AA-1)
Objective: minimize re-annotation cost by adapting existing annotator guides and lightweight retraining instead of wholesale relabels when QSPI indicates localized degradation.
Steps (numbered runbook):
1. Trigger: QSPI < 95 for a candidate artifact. Create a QSPI incident in the evaluation queue.
2. Automated triage: run per-slice delta analysis to produce a prioritized table: slice, delta-score, top failure-mode (e.g., omission, hallucination, schema drift).
3. Rapid annotation rehearsal (2-day window): sample up to 50 failing probes per top-5 slices and run a calibration session with annotators. Use the following checklist during rehearsal:
   - Present side-by-side baseline vs candidate responses.
   - Annotators flag root-cause (formatting vs content vs predicate mismatch).
   - Track time-per-annotation and inter-annotator-agreement (IAA).
4. Adaptation decision matrix:
   - If IAA >= 0.8 and time-per-annotation <= baseline * 1.2, proceed to targeted guide updates and re-run QSPI.
   - If IAA < 0.6 or time > baseline * 1.5, escalate to model-team for quantization fallback or refined kernel selection.
5. If targeted guide updates lead to QSPI >= 97, proceed with promotion. Otherwise, follow rollback path.

Annotator training artifacts to generate:
- Short "delta guide" (1 page) describing specific failure patterns and canonical corrections.
- 10-shot micro-training bundle (JSONL) for retraining lightweight classifier head or annotation heuristics.
- Annotator time-cost estimate and re-run probes list.

4) Thresholds, SLOs, and gating rules
- Gate levels (default):
  - Safe: QSPI >= 98 — automatic promotion allowed after automated acceptance suite passes.
  - Caution: QSPI 95-97 — require AA-1 and QSPI re-evaluation.
  - Block: QSPI < 95 — block promotion, require model-team investigation or fallback to higher-precision artifact.
- Production alerting: if promoted artifact later records a slice-level drift (slice delta > 0.12 in live telemetry) within first 72 hours, open P0 evaluation and auto-rollforward to pre-quantized variant.

5) Triage and rollback playbook (step-by-step with CLI examples)
Runbook summary: identify affected slices, run fast-scan, apply AA-1 rehearsal, and either remediate or rollback.
Example commands (eval harness CLI):
- Run QSPI on candidate:
  rwd-eval qspi --baseline=artifact:float-v2 --candidate=artifact:quant-4bit-v1 --probe-set=qspi-top50 --out=qspi-report.json
- Generate per-slice deltas:
  rwd-eval slice-diff --input=qspi-report.json --format=table > slice-deltas.csv
- Launch annotator rehearsal (scheduler API):
  rwd-ops schedule-rehearsal --slices slice-deltas.csv --annotator-pool=annotators-core --ttl=48h
Rollback / rollforward:
- If rollback required:
  rwd-deploy promote --artifact=artifact:float-v2 --route=canary --reason=\"QSPI-block: <95\"
- If rollforward after fix:
  rwd-deploy promote --artifact=artifact:quant-4bit-v1 --route=canary --notes=\"QSPI remediated via AA-1; QSPI=97.8\"

6) Observability and dashboarding recommendations
Minimum telemetry to publish on model-promotions dashboard:
- QSPI overall score (trend, last 30 days)
- Per-slice s_i and weight w_i trendlines
- Annotator metrics: IAA (krippendorff alpha), median time-per-annotation, training bundle reuse count
- Production feedback loop: slice-level user complaint rate and automatic rollback triggers
Suggested dashboard panels:
| Panel | Purpose | Owner | SLO |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| QSPI trend | Track artifact drift across promotions | Applied ML | >98 day-over-day | 
| Slice heatmap | Highlight concentrated losses | Model SRE | per-slice alert if delta > 0.12 |
| Annotator health | Track IAA and throughput | Annotation Ops | IAA >= 0.75 |

7) Ownership and decision matrix
- Primary owners:
  - Metric steward: Applied ML (Priya Natarajan) — maintains QSPI spec and default alpha/weights.
  - Operational steward: Annotation Ops (María Alvarez) — runs AA-1 and trains annotators.
  - Promotion gate owner: Model Release (Ethan Cole) — final promotion authority.
- Escalation path: If model-team disagrees with AA-1 outcome, open an evaluation scoping ticket and convene a cross-functional review within 48 hours.

Appendices
A) YAML manifest example for probe set specification:
---
probe_set_name: qspi-top50
probes: 50
mix: {production_mirror: 0.6, synthetic_edge: 0.3, canonical: 0.1}
weighting: {financial: 2.0, functioncall: 1.5, conversational: 0.8}
alpha: 1.0
---

B) Quick reference: remediation decision table
| Condition | Action |
| QSPI >= 98 | Auto-promote after automated checks |
| 95 <= QSPI < 98 and AA-1 passes | Targeted promotion with annotation guardrail |
| QSPI < 95 | Block promotion and open quant-investigation |

C) Example JSON snippet for micro-training bundle (1-line):
{"prompt": "User: Extract amount and currency. Text: \"Transfer $20 to savings.\"", "label": {"amount": 20, "currency": "USD"}}

D) Glossary:
- QSPI: Quantization Signal Preservation Index
- AA-1: Annotator Adaptation Protocol version 1
- IAA: Inter-Annotator Agreement

Related work and references:
- See the quantization-profiles folder for kernel-specific notes and existing probe manifests.
- See eval-harness/quantized-artifact-eval-gate-and-rollout-2026 for integration with CI gating.

Questions / feedback:
- Ping @priya.natarajan on Slack (#applied-ml) for metric changes or sample-set requests.

Document history:
- 2026-01-12: Draft created by Priya Natarajan
- 2026-02-10: Added AA-1 protocol after annotator rehearsal pilot (María Alvarez)
- 2026-03-01: Published; thresholds adjusted after shadow-run results (Ethan Cole)

title: Service boundary evaluation and quantization migration checklist
preview:
Overview

This checklist defines the cross-functional operational playbook for evaluating service boundaries and migrating model serving from floating-point artifacts to quantized artifacts (8-bit, 4-bit, mixed) with minimal user-facing regression risk. The goal is to ensure predictable latency/cost improvements while preserving quality and observability contracts. This document ties together: eval-harness checks, prompt and benchmark selection, model onboarding gates, quality guardrails, quantization profile validation, and regression triage steps.

Scope

- Applies to any model promotion that changes runtime numeric representation (FP16/FP32 -> INT8/PACT/4-bit) for production endpoints (hosted, dedicated, or private).
- Includes single-model migrations and model-variant rollouts where a quantized variant will be routed by policy.
- Excludes research-only artifacts and offline-only embeddings refreshes.

Primary owners and contacts

- Service owner: Priya Natarajan (ML Infra / Applied ML) - priya@redwood.example.com
- SRE/Performance owner: Jamal Carter - jamal@redwood.example.com
- Evaluation lead: Ethan Miller - ethan@redwood.example.com

Priorities and acceptance criteria (summary)

- Safety/Quality: Zero critical semantic regressions on production prompt families; no increase in severity-1 (S1) safety sentinels.
- Latency: P95 token generation latency should not exceed baseline by more than 10% under nominal load.
- Cost: Expected per-token cost reduction (est. 20–60%) must be validated in a 24h shadow run.
- Observability: Traces, token-level cost breakdowns, and eval-sentinels must be active and firing against baseline slices.

Pre-migration checklist (must be satisfied before building quant artifacts)

1) Workload and boundary analysis
   - Identify the service boundary (API route/model key) and all downstream consumers.
   - Produce a baseline workload fingerprint: token length distribution, function-calls ratio, streaming vs batch, cold start rate.
   - Tag the route with a migration candidate label in the config catalog.

2) Prompt & benchmark selection
   - Select a representative probe set (recommended 5k–20k probes) split across: high-traffic slices, safety-sensitive prompts, long-context prompts, multi-turn threads, and rare edgecases.
   - For each probe include: canonical prompt, expected anchor tokens, pass/fail sentinel, and a golden response reference where applicable.

3) Eval harness configuration
   - Configure a dedicated eval job with: deterministic seeds, temperature and max_tokens matching production, and tokenization parity checks.
   - Enable both delta-metrics and qualitative human review queues for slices with >X% regression (X default 5%).

Quant profiling & artifact build checklist

- Quant profile selection: list candidate quant profiles (per-layer or per-kernel) and annotate tradeoffs. Example table: 

  | Candidate | Precision | Notes | Expected cost delta | Owner |
  |-----------|-----------|-------|---------------------|-------|
  | qp-int8   | 8-bit     | Default per-tensor symmetric | -40% | infra-quant |
  | qp-4bit   | 4-bit     | Aggressive, requires KV-cache tuning | -60% | infra-quant |
  | mixed-8-4 | hybrid    | FP16 for attention, 4-bit for FFN | -50% | infra-quant |

- Locally validate tensor parity (per-layer L2 and activation distribution checks) and record artifacts in the quant-catalog location.
- Attach quant metadata to model manifest: quant_profile_id, sample-seed, compressor-version, and build-recipe id.

Shadow/validation stage (golden path)

1) Shadow run (no traffic impact):
   - Run the quantized model in shadow for 24–72 hours against live traffic sampling (recommended 2–10% traffic sample).
   - Capture the following telemetry for both baseline and quantized variant: token-level latency (p50/p95/p99), response-stability-index (RSI), embedding cosine drift on embedding routes, safety sentinel hit-rate, and cost per token.

2) Quality gating using eval harness:
   - Delta metrics to compute: exact-match rate, semantic-similarity delta (BERTScore or proprietary similarity), hallucination proxy (unexpected-entity-rate), and RSI.
   - Gate criteria (default):
     * semantic-similarity delta <= 2.5 absolute points on production slices
     * RSI drop <= 0.03
     * Safety sentinel rate not increased by more than 0.1x absolute
     * Latency p95 regression <= 10%

3) Human-in-the-loop review
   - For slices failing any gate, auto-queue for annotator triage with a sample size of 200 examples or until 95% confidence is reached.

Canary rollout and progressive promotion

1) Micro-canary (1–2% traffic) for 2–4 hours
   - Route a small percentage of traffic via routing policy to quantized variant. Monitor SLOs and quality-sentinels.

2) Macro-canary (5–15% traffic) for 24 hours
   - Increase traffic and run full pipeline (eval harness repeat on live samples + human review of failure samples).

3) Gradual promotion (increments of 10–20% every 12–24h)
   - Stop and roll back if: any S1 sentinels are triggered, semantic-similarity delta exceeds threshold, or latency p95 increases beyond escalation threshold.

Rollback criteria and emergency process

- Immediately rollback if any of the following occur: S1 safety sentinel, customer-facing error-rate > 0.5% absolute increase, or unresolvable semantic regressions on a critical slice.
- Triage steps in rollback event:
  1) Capture failing request IDs and attach full traces + model-side logs.
  2) Run targeted eval-harness replay of failing probes across baseline and candidate quant-artifact.
  3) If root cause is quantization, escalate to infra-quant and model-onboarding owners; prepare hot-fix (less aggressive quant profile or mixed-precision fallback).

Regression triage quickplay (for triage teams)

- Initial assessment (first 30 minutes):
  * Confirm whether regression is reproducible on the eval-harness using the failing probe set.
  * Check KV-cache alignment and tokenization parity for the failing requests.

- Bisect matrix:
  * Axis 1: quant_profile (int8, 4bit, mixed)
  * Axis 2: sequence length bucket (short/medium/long)
  * Axis 3: temperature/decoding settings
  Run a batched grid to isolate when the failure appears.

- Rapid mitigation patterns:
  1) Route failing slices to baseline float model via routing policy.
  2) Enable per-route quant-fallback setting (prefer mixed-precision) for affected model key.
  3) Open a regression incident with title prefixed: [REGRESSION][quant] <service-route> and assign to infra-quant.

Operational dashboards and alerts (minimum required)

- Dashboards:
  * Migration overview: traffic split, P50/P95 latency baseline vs quant, cost per token, safety sentinel delta, RSI trend.
  * Slice heatmap: top 20 slices by volume and quality delta.

- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  * Quality delta alert: semantic-similarity delta > 2.5 points (severity P2)
  * Safety sentinel increase: absolute increase > 0.1% (severity P1)
  * Latency regression: p95 > baseline * 1.10 (severity P2)
  * Unexpected error-rate: > 0.5% absolute (severity P1)

Post-promotion verification and documentation

- Run a 7-day monitored window where weekly aggregated metrics are reviewed by the service owner and evaluation lead.
- Update model manifest and production catalog with final quant_profile, effective date, and observed cost savings.
- Publish a short post-mortem or migration summary including: decision rationale, measured deltas, any rollback incidents, and recommended next steps for future migrations.

Appendix: example commands and snippets

- Eval harness run (example):
  > ./eval-harness run --manifest manifests/service-xyz.json --probes probes/service-xyz/production-probes.json --seed 42 --compare-to baseline-artifact-id

- Example routing policy (YAML excerpt):

  route: /v1/generate/service-xyz
  variants:
    - id: baseline-fp16
      weight: 80
    - id: quant-qp-int8
      weight: 20
  fallback_policy: route-to-baseline-on-sentinel

- Example quick-check JSON for similarity metric: 
  {
    "probe_id": "12345",
    "baseline_embedding_cos": 0.9234,
    "quant_embedding_cos": 0.8952,
    "similarity_delta": -0.0282
  }

Related policies and references

- See the quantization profile library for approved profiles and binary provenance.
- Follow the eval-harness seeding and determinism rules when building comparison runs.
- Use the regression-triage incident template when opening incidents for production regressions.

Notes and future work

- Next iteration: automate quant-profile A/B scoring via the eval-harness and integrate with rollout policy to support automated gradual promotion when deltas remain within a conservative band.
- Investigate more robust embedding-drift alarms that map directly to downstream retrieval quality impact.

Shadow/validation stage (golden path)

1) Shadow run (no traffic impact):
   - Run the quantized model in shadow for 24–72 hours against live traffic sampling (recommended 2–10% traffic sample).
   - Capture the following telemetry for both baseline and quantized variant: token-level latency (p50/p95/p99), response-stability-index (RSI), embedding cosine drift on embedding routes, safety sentinel hit-rate, and cost per token.

2) Quality gating using eval harness:
   - Delta metrics to compute: exact-match rate, semantic-similarity delta (BERTScore or proprietary similarity), hallucination proxy (unexpected-entity-rate), and RSI.
   - Gate criteria (default):
     * semantic-similarity delta <= 2.5 absolute points on production slices
     * RSI drop <= 0.03
     * Safety sentinel rate not increased by more than 0.1x absolute
     * Latency p95 regression <= 10%

3) Human-in-the-loop review
   - For slices failing any gate, auto-queue for annotator triage with a sample size of 200 examples or until 95% confidence is reached.

Canary rollout and progressive promotion

1) Micro-canary (1–2% traffic) for 2–4 hours
   - Route a small percentage of traffic via routing policy to quantized variant. Monitor SLOs and quality-sentinels.

2) Macro-canary (5–15% traffic) for 24 hours
   - Increase traffic and run full pipeline (eval harness repeat on live samples + human review of failure samples).

3) Gradual promotion (increments of 10–20% every 12–24h)
   - Stop and roll back if: any S1 sentinels are triggered, semantic-similarity delta exceeds threshold, or latency p95 increases beyond escalation threshold.

Rollback criteria and emergency process

- Immediately rollback if any of the following occur: S1 safety sentinel, customer-facing error-rate > 0.5% absolute increase, or unresolvable semantic regressions on a critical slice.
- Triage steps in rollback event:
  1) Capture failing request IDs and attach full traces + model-side logs.
  2) Run targeted eval-harness replay of failing probes across baseline and candidate quant-artifact.
  3) If root cause is quantization, escalate to infra-quant and model-onboarding owners; prepare hot-fix (less aggressive quant profile or mixed-precision fallback).

Regression triage quickplay (for triage teams)

- Initial assessment (first 30 minutes):
  * Confirm whether regression is reproducible on the eval-harness using the failing probe set.
  * Check KV-cache alignment and tokenization parity for the failing requests.

- Bisect matrix:
  * Axis 1: quant_profile (int8, 4bit, mixed)
  * Axis 2: sequence length bucket (short/medium/long)
  * Axis 3: temperature/decoding settings
  Run a batched grid to isolate when the failure appears.

- Rapid mitigation patterns:
  1) Route failing slices to baseline float model via routing policy.
  2) Enable per-route quant-fallback setting (prefer mixed-precision) for affected model key.
  3) Open a regression incident with title prefixed: [REGRESSION][quant] <service-route> and assign to infra-quant.

Operational dashboards and alerts (minimum required)

- Dashboards:
  * Migration overview: traffic split, P50/P95 latency baseline vs quant, cost per token, safety sentinel delta, RSI trend.
  * Slice heatmap: top 20 slices by volume and quality delta.

- Alerts (recommended thresholds):
  * Quality delta alert: semantic-similarity delta > 2.5 points (severity P2)
  * Safety sentinel increase: absolute increase > 0.1% (severity P1)
  * Latency regression: p95 > baseline * 1.10 (severity P2)
  * Unexpected error-rate: > 0.5% absolute (severity P1)

Post-promotion verification and documentation

- Run a 7-day monitored window where weekly aggregated metrics are reviewed by the service owner and evaluation lead.
- Update model manifest and production catalog with final quant_profile, effective date, and observed cost savings.
- Publish a short post-mortem or migration summary including: decision rationale, measured deltas, any rollback incidents, and recommended next steps for future migrations.

Appendix: example commands and snippets

- Eval harness run (example):
  > ./eval-harness run --manifest manifests/service-xyz.json --probes probes/service-xyz/production-probes.json --seed 42 --compare-to baseline-artifact-id

- Example routing policy (YAML excerpt):

  route: /v1/generate/service-xyz
  variants:
    - id: baseline-fp16
      weight: 80
    - id: quant-qp-int8
      weight: 20
  fallback_policy: route-to-baseline-on-sentinel

- Example quick-check JSON for similarity metric: 
  {
    "probe_id": "12345",
    "baseline_embedding_cos": 0.9234,
    "quant_embedding_cos": 0.8952,
    "similarity_delta": -0.0282
  }

Related policies and references

- See the quantization profile library for approved profiles and binary provenance.
- Follow the eval-harness seeding and determinism rules when building comparison runs.
- Use the regression-triage incident template when opening incidents for production regressions.

Notes and future work

- Next iteration: automate quant-profile A/B scoring via the eval-harness and integrate with rollout policy to support automated gradual promotion when deltas remain within a conservative band.
- Investigate more robust embedding-drift alarms that map directly to downstream retrieval quality impact.

title: Eval cost slice prioritization and resource budgeting
preview:
Summary

This document defines a prioritization and budgeting framework for running slice-driven evaluations in Redwood’s eval harness. It describes a deterministic scoring function for slice prioritization, recommended resource budgets for common evaluation classes (smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan), integration points with quantization profiles and onboarding, and a triage fast-path for regression detection and mitigation.

Why this exists

- Eval budgets are finite: the eval harness runs thousands of slices across many models and releases. Uncontrolled execution causes high cost and slow feedback.
- We need consistent signal for promotion and regression triage while minimizing GPU/CPU spend.
- Quantization changes and heterogeneous deployments (private/dedicated) require different resource profiles; this doc standardizes budgeting and prioritization so teams follow predictable least-cost-first patterns.

Scope

- Applies to all automated and scheduled evaluation jobs executed via the eval harness (interactive, scheduled, and triggered by model promotion pipelines).
- Does NOT cover human labeling budgets or external A/B experiments (separate playbooks exist).

Definitions

- Slice: atomic workload segment (e.g., subset of prompts with a common characteristic).
- Eval class: smoke, micro-canary, full-slice, long-tail scan.
- Cost unit: GPU-hour equivalent at Redwood internal rate (example: 1 A100-80GB hour = 1.0 cost unit; 1 T4/8GB hour = 0.08 cost units).
- Priority window: timeframe within which a slice should be executed relative to model event (e.g., within 2 hours for micro-canary).

Priority scoring algorithm (deterministic)

We compute a score S for each slice to decide execution order when demand exceeds capacity. Higher S runs first.

Formula: S = W_i * I + W_r * R + W_m * M + W_c * (1 / (1 + C))

Where:
- I (impact) in [0,1]: business/feature impact heuristic (0 low, 1 high). Set by owning team in slice metadata.
- R (regression-signal) in [0,1]: recent historical alert density for this slice (0 none, 1 frequent).
- M (model-change): binary 1 if model architecture/quantization variant changed since last passing run, else 0.
- C (cost-estimate): predicted cost in cost units for full slice run.
- W_i, W_r, W_m, W_c = weights default = [0.45, 0.25, 0.20, 0.10]. Weights are tunable via eval-harness config.

Score interpretation:
- S >= 0.7: high priority (run immediately; consider parallelization).
- 0.4 <= S < 0.7: medium (schedule within the next resource window).
- S < 0.4: low (defer to overnight/backfill).

Priority components table

| Component | Range | Source | Default weight | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|
| Impact (I) | 0.0 - 1.0 | Owner metadata | 0.45 | Product/PM sets impact; engineering may override for infra-critical slices |
| Regression (R) | 0.0 - 1.0 | Telemetry (alerts/sec, failed-runs) | 0.25 | Smoothed over last 30 days |
| Model-change (M) | 0 or 1 | CI metadata | 0.20 | Includes quantization and kernel switches |
| Cost (C) | >0 | Eval cost estimator | 0.10 | Normalized to cost units; inverted in score to prefer low-cost slices |

Resource budgeting (default budgets)

These are guidance values. Teams may request overrides for high-value flows; override requests go to eng-applied-ml with cost justification. Budget values assume Redwood internal cost unit; convert per-region via pricing table in runbook.

| Eval class | Target wall-clock | Max concurrent workers | Max GPU-hours per run | Example use-cases |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Smoke | 5m | 1 | 0.02 | Pre-commit/quick sanity checks |
| Micro-canary | 30m | 2 | 0.6 | Canary of new model variant on top 10 slices |
| Full-slice | 2-6h | 10 | 8.0 | Promotion gating for model families |
| Long-tail scan | 24h | 4 | 24.0 | Rare-case stress and drift detection |

Budget enforcement rules

1. Each eval job must declare eval_class and estimator.cost_units in job YAML. The scheduler will reject jobs exceeding per-team daily caps.
2. Peak concurrency throttles: when global cost consumption in last 1 hour > 80% of pool capacity, only high-priority (S >= 0.7) slices are scheduled until consumption drops below 60%.
3. Quantized runs count as a separate cost bucket and may require additional shadow runs; treat them as model-change (M=1) for initial prioritization.

YAML: minimal eval job example

---
# eval-job.yaml
job_name: email-intent-full-slice
eval_class: full-slice
owner: \"nlp-team\"
priority_metadata:
  impact: 0.8
  model_change: true
estimator:
  cost_units: 7.5
  gpu_type: a100-80gb
  expected_wall_minutes: 240
---

Scheduling procedure (step-by-step)

1. When a model-promotion event occurs, the promotion orchestration writes a scheduling request for the required slices. Include eval_class, owner, and priority_metadata.
2. The eval-scheduler computes S for each slice and places slices into priority queues.
3. The scheduler enforces daily budget caps per team; if cap exceeded, scheduler downgrades non-critical slices with an annotation and notifies owners.
4. For micro-canaries, the scheduler ensures runs complete within the priority window (default 2 hours). If a micro-canary fails (see acceptance thresholds below), the pipeline pauses and triggers the regression triage fast-path.

Acceptance thresholds and sample sizes

- Smoke: qualitative checks; failure on runtime error or >5% invalid responses.
- Micro-canary: run N=100 samples; acceptance if metric delta <= 1.5x baseline variance for primary metric OR absolute metric >= baseline - epsilon.
- Full-slice: sample size determined by slice variance profiling; baseline formula: N = min(5000, ceil( (Z^2 * sigma^2) / d^2 )), where Z=1.96 (95% CI), sigma is observed stddev from historical runs, d is acceptable absolute difference. Default N=1000 if no history.

Integration with quantization profiles and model onboarding

- Onboarding pipelines must tag any change to quant_profile_id. That tag sets model_change=M for the first subsequent eval run and prioritizes slices accordingly.
- For quantization-induced runs, run two lanes in parallel (float baseline shadow + quantized variant) for the micro-canary. Cost estimation should account for both lanes.

Regression triage fast-path

When a micro-canary or full-slice run exceeds failure thresholds, the following fast-path is executed:

1. Pause promotion and mark model as BLOCKED.
2. Snapshot telemetry and store a prioritized subset: top 100 failed prompts, token-level traces, KV-cache rollback events, and runtimes.
3. Run automated bisect across variants: kernel selection, quant profile, model weights (if multi-version) — limit to 3 parallel bisect branches.
4. If bisect isolates a single cause with confidence > 90% (see bisect spec), auto-open a regression incident with severity determined by impacted SLOs.
5. If cause not isolated within 60 minutes, run fallback: promote earlier verified model variant and schedule detailed offline triage with ML owners.

Telemetry and observability requirements

Eval runs must emit the following fields at 1-minute granularity:
- eval_job_id, slice_id, model_version, quant_profile_id, worker_id, gpu_type, tokens_generated, latency_p50/p95/p99, error_count, failed_prompts_count, metric_values (primary/secondary), cost_units_consumed.

Dashboard and alerts

- A dedicated dashboard \"Eval Cost & Priority\" lists active jobs, S scores, and cost burn rate.
- Alert: when per-team cost consumption >= 90% of daily cap -> page on-call for that team.
- Alert: micro-canary failure -> slack #model-promotions and create incident-tmp channel via API.

Governance and ownership

- Owning team: declares impact and maintains slice metadata; responsible for justifying budget overrides.
- Platform: eng-applied-ml operates scheduler, maintains cost model, and reviews monthly budget exceptions.
- SRE: enforces pool concurrency caps and adjusts rate-limiting during global incidents.

Operational runbook (quick actions)

- If a slice is consuming unexpected cost (>2x estimate):
  1. Cancel active job via evalctl: evalctl cancel <job_id>.
  2. Inspect estimator outputs: evalctl inspect <job_id> --fields estimator.*.
  3. If estimator was stale, file a ticket to eng-applied-ml with the job snapshot.

- Triaging a micro-canary regression:
  1. Lock promotion and run automated bisect: evalctl bisect --job <job_id> --variants quant,kernel,weights
  2. If bisect identifies variant: rollback quant profile via model-registry UI and rerun micro-canary.

Example CLI commands

# schedule a micro-canary
evalctl schedule --job-file eval-job.yaml --priority now
# cancel a running job
evalctl cancel --job-id 2026-AB12
# fetch diagnostics for a completed run
evalctl fetch --job-id 2026-AB12 --artifact traces/*.json

Security and cost accounting

- All eval artifacts containing customer prompt examples must be labeled PII/TOS compliant at creation. The scheduler will reject runs labeled \"customer-sensitive\" unless confidentiality override is granted.
- Cost attribution is exported daily to finance with the following dimensions: team, owner, model_family, quant_profile_id, region.

Change log

- 2026-02-12 (Ava Chen): Initial publish.
- 2026-03-01 (Ava Chen): Weight defaults tuned from internal pilot; added triage bisect timebox and CLI examples.

Related runbooks and references

- eval-harness/runbooks/threshold-tuning (for how to set sigma and d)
- applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quant-profile-synthesis-and-safety-playbook-2024 (quant change handling)
- applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/production-model-certification-checklist-2026 (promotion gating)

For questions or budget override requests, email eng-applied-ml@redwood.com or open a PR to the eval-harness scheduler config repository.
