title: Savings Estimation Validation Plan (Historical Telemetry) — Optimize Suggestions Beta
preview:
h1. Purpose
This document defines how we will validate the *savings estimator v0* for the Console “Optimization suggestions” beta (batching + caching only) using *historical telemetry*. The goal is to quantify estimation quality, characterize failure modes, and set *acceptance thresholds* for beta launch/expansion.

This is *not* a customer commitment document. All outputs are “estimated savings” and must remain conservative and well-explained in-product.

h1. Scope
h2. In scope (beta)
* Savings estimation for:
  * *Batching suggestions* (continuous batching / max batch size / target batch latency window)
  * *Caching suggestions* (prefix/KV caching enablement and cache policy parameters)
* Metrics for:
  * *Cost impact* (token cost / GPU-seconds proxy where available)
  * *Latency impact* (p50/p95 deltas)
  * *Confidence inputs* (telemetry coverage, variance, stability)

h2. Out of scope (beta)
* Quantization recommendations
* Automatic application without user confirmation
* Customer-specific guarantees or contractually binding savings

h1. Owners and stakeholders
* Primary owner: Jonas Weber (Applied ML / perf & evals)
* Estimator implementation: Hassan Mirza (Batching/caching modeling), Sana Farid (confidence + error analysis)
* Telemetry & cost attribution inputs: Nadia Rahman (cost pipeline), Logan Wright (telemetry platform)
* Product stakeholders: Aditya Rao (Optimize), Marissa Cole (Console)

h1. What we are validating
h2. Estimator outputs (per suggestion)
Each suggestion (route × model × region × deployment mode) produces:
* Estimated savings:
  * $/day and % reduction (relative to current configuration)
* Estimated performance impact:
  * p50 and p95 latency deltas (ms)
* Risk label:
  * Low / Medium / High (validated separately; see ADR)
* Confidence signals:
  * Telemetry coverage window, variance, stability, and staleness timestamp

h2. Definition of “ground truth” for validation
We use *counterfactual approximations* and *observed before/after deltas* where possible:
1) *Natural experiments* (preferred): routes where configuration changed historically (e.g., caching enabled, batch params adjusted) with sufficient pre/post traffic.
2) *A/B or canary windows* (when available): explicit rollout periods with known change timestamps.
3) *Proxy replay* (fallback): simulate expected improvements using logged distributions and validated microbenchmarks for the same model/hardware class. (Used to bound error; not considered “observed truth”.)

We will label each validation example with a *truth tier* (T1/T2/T3) and report metrics by tier.

h1. Data sources and eligibility
h2. Telemetry sources
* Request aggregates per route/model/region:
  * QPS, concurrency distribution
  * Input/output token counts (p50/p95/p99)
  * Sequence length histograms
  * Latency distributions (p50/p95/p99)
  * Error rate (5xx/429/timeouts)
* Cache metrics (when enabled):
  * Cache hit rate, prefix match rate, eviction rate
  * Cache read/write latency (if instrumented)
* Cost attribution:
  * Per-route cost estimate ($) and/or GPU time proxy, aligned to billing window

h2. Validation window
* Default historical window: last 28 days
* Minimum traffic for inclusion:
  * ≥ 50k requests in the baseline window *and*
  * ≥ 7 distinct days with traffic (to avoid one-off spikes)
* Minimum stability:
  * Baseline p95 latency coefficient of variation (CV) < 0.35 (unless explicitly investigating unstable routes)

h2. Exclusions
* Routes with known incident periods in the baseline or post-change window (unless the change itself is part of incident mitigation)
* Routes with major model version changes confounded with the optimization change (unless we can isolate via pinned model ID)

h1. Sampling strategy
We will build a validation set that is representative and stress-tests edge cases.

h2. Sampling frame
All orgs/projects enrolled in dogfood + selected beta candidates with adequate telemetry maturity.

We stratify by:
* Deployment mode: Hosted / Dedicated / Private (if telemetry is available)
* Model family: Llama-class vs Mistral-class vs other curated open models
* Route type: chat/generate vs embeddings (embeddings generally excluded for batching/caching suggestions beta)
* Region
* Traffic level: high (top 20%), mid (middle 60%), low (bottom 20% but meeting minimum traffic)
* Latency sensitivity: inferred from configured SLO tier (if available) or baseline p95

h2. Sample sizes (targets)
* T1 (natural experiment pre/post): 60 route-model pairs
* T2 (explicit rollout/A-B): 20 route-model pairs
* T3 (proxy replay only): 40 route-model pairs (used for bounding and regression testing)

Total target: 120 validation examples.

h2. Over-sampling known risky areas
We will intentionally include:
* Routes with high variance in input token length
* Routes with spiky concurrency
* Routes with high tail-latency sensitivity (baseline p95 > 2.5× p50)

h1. Methodology
h2. Pre/post analysis (T1/T2)
For each example, define:
* Baseline window: 7 days ending 24h before change
* Post window: 7 days starting 24h after change
(We add the 24h buffer to reduce effects of partial rollouts and cache warm-up.)

Compute observed deltas:
* Cost delta:
  * Δ$/request and Δ$/day (normalized by request volume)
* Latency delta:
  * Δp50, Δp95 (ms)
* Secondary checks:
  * Error rate change (timeouts/5xx), rate limiting events (429)

h2. Matching / confound control
To reduce bias from traffic drift:
* Match baseline and post periods by day-of-week and hour-of-day where possible.
* Reweight by input/output token distributions:
  * If post window has materially different token mix (JS divergence > 0.10), label as “token-shift confounded” and report separately.
* Exclude windows overlapping platform incidents or major capacity changes.

h2. Proxy replay (T3)
Use telemetry distributions (tokens, concurrency) and a hardware/model performance profile to predict:
* Expected throughput change
* Expected latency shift under batching/caching knobs

T3 is used to:
* Detect regressions across estimator versions
* Provide conservative bounds when no pre/post truth exists

h1. Error metrics and reporting
We report metrics at two levels: *per-suggestion* and *portfolio* (top-N suggestions).

h2. Primary error metrics (cost)
For each example i:
* Absolute error (AE): |ŝ_i − s_i|
* Relative error (RE): (ŝ_i − s_i) / max(|s_i|, ε)
  * Use ε = $10/day equivalent to avoid divide-by-near-zero blowups
* Weighted MAPE (wMAPE): Σ|ŝ_i − s_i| / Σ|s_i|
* Bias: mean(ŝ_i − s_i)

Where:
* ŝ_i = estimated savings ($/day)
* s_i = observed savings ($/day) from pre/post normalization

h2. Primary error metrics (latency)
* MAE for Δp50 and Δp95 (ms)
* Tail safety rate:
  * % examples where estimator predicts Δp95 ≤ +X ms but observed Δp95 > +X ms
  * We evaluate X ∈ {25ms, 50ms, 100ms} depending on latency tier

h2. Calibration / confidence evaluation
If estimator emits confidence bands or a confidence score:
* Coverage:
  * % of examples where observed savings falls within predicted [low, high] interval
* Monotonicity:
  * Higher confidence buckets should have lower wMAPE

h2. “Top opportunities” usefulness metric
Since the Console surfaces top opportunities, we evaluate ranking quality:
* Precision@K (K=5,10): fraction of top-K estimated savings suggestions that are also top-K observed savings (within tier)
* NDCG@K for ranking alignment

h2. Reporting slices
We will publish metrics by:
* Suggestion type: batching vs caching
* Truth tier: T1/T2/T3
* Traffic level and variance
* Model family and region

h1. Acceptance thresholds (beta)
These thresholds gate *beta expansion* (not dogfood experimentation).

h2. Cost estimation thresholds
For T1/T2 combined:
* wMAPE (savings $/day):
  * ≤ 35% overall
  * ≤ 45% in each major slice (batching/caching)
* Bias (mean ŝ − s):
  * ≤ +10% of mean observed savings (we prefer slight underestimation; any consistent overestimation must be investigated)
* Overestimation guardrail:
  * P(ŝ > 1.5× s) ≤ 20% for examples with s ≥ $50/day

h2. Latency impact thresholds
For T1/T2 combined:
* MAE(Δp95): ≤ 40ms overall
* Tail safety:
  * For routes labeled Low risk, observed Δp95 > +50ms should occur in ≤ 5% of examples
  * For Medium risk, observed Δp95 > +100ms should occur in ≤ 10% of examples

h2. Confidence and interval quality
If intervals are shown in-product for beta:
* 80% predicted intervals should have ≥ 75% empirical coverage (we accept slight under-coverage in beta but must track)
* Confidence buckets must be monotonic: highest-confidence bucket wMAPE ≤ lowest-confidence bucket wMAPE − 10pp

h2. Console safety requirements (non-metric)
* For any suggestion where telemetry coverage is insufficient (e.g., < 7 days or missing cache signals), the UI must:
  * downgrade confidence,
  * show “insufficient data” caveat, or
  * suppress the suggestion per guardrails.

h1. Beta GA criteria (forward-looking, not required for initial beta)
We will revisit after initial customer feedback, but current targets for GA:
* Cost wMAPE ≤ 25% (T1/T2)
* Overestimation rate P(ŝ > 1.5× s) ≤ 10% for s ≥ $50/day
* MAE(Δp95) ≤ 25ms (T1/T2)
* Clear calibration with published confidence definitions and stable coverage

h1. Execution plan
h2. Deliverables
1) Validation dataset manifest
* List of route/model/org identifiers (pseudonymized), truth tier, change timestamps, eligibility flags.
2) Reproducible evaluation notebook/pipeline
* Produces metric tables and slice dashboards.
3) Findings report
* Summary, key failure modes, recommended estimator adjustments and UI guardrails.

h2. Timeline
* Week 1: Build candidate list and truth-tier labeling; align with telemetry owners on availability.
* Week 2: Run T1/T2 evaluation; compute metrics and slices; identify systemic overestimation.
* Week 3: Patch estimator and re-run; finalize beta acceptance recommendation.

h1. Known failure modes to watch
* Token-shift confounding: post window has different prompt mix.
* Cache warm-up effects: early post window may understate benefits (or overstate if warmed by prior traffic).
* Hidden throttling: concurrency caps or rate-limits change between windows.
* Multi-change confounds: model version, kernel/runtime upgrade, or region capacity change coincident with config change.

Mitigations:
* Buffer windows around changes
* Label and report confounded examples rather than hiding them
* Conservative UI messaging when variance is high

h1. Documentation and customer-facing implications
* If we fail acceptance thresholds:
  * Do not expand beta cohort.
  * Tighten guardrails (suppress low-confidence suggestions) and adjust copy to emphasize uncertainty.
* If we pass thresholds:
  * Proceed with staged beta expansion, but continue to log discrepancies and follow-up with customers reporting mismatches.

h1. Appendix A: Metric definitions (quick reference)
* wMAPE: Σ|error| / Σ|truth|
* MAE: mean(|prediction − truth|)
* Precision@K: overlap of top-K predicted vs top-K truth
* NDCG@K: ranking quality accounting for position

h1. Appendix B: Operational notes
* All analyses must use org/project-scoped data access and comply with telemetry privacy guidelines.
* Any use of customer identifiers in analysis artifacts must be redacted or replaced with stable internal IDs before sharing outside Applied ML/Optimize engineering.
text:
h1. Purpose
This document defines how we will validate the *savings estimator v0* for the Console “Optimization suggestions” beta (batching + caching only) using *historical telemetry*. The goal is to quantify estimation quality, characterize failure modes, and set *acceptance thresholds* for beta launch/expansion.

This is *not* a customer commitment document. All outputs are “estimated savings” and must remain conservative and well-explained in-product.

h1. Scope
h2. In scope (beta)
* Savings estimation for:
  * *Batching suggestions* (continuous batching / max batch size / target batch latency window)
  * *Caching suggestions* (prefix/KV caching enablement and cache policy parameters)
* Metrics for:
  * *Cost impact* (token cost / GPU-seconds proxy where available)
  * *Latency impact* (p50/p95 deltas)
  * *Confidence inputs* (telemetry coverage, variance, stability)

h2. Out of scope (beta)
* Quantization recommendations
* Automatic application without user confirmation
* Customer-specific guarantees or contractually binding savings

h1. Owners and stakeholders
* Primary owner: Jonas Weber (Applied ML / perf & evals)
* Estimator implementation: Hassan Mirza (Batching/caching modeling), Sana Farid (confidence + error analysis)
* Telemetry & cost attribution inputs: Nadia Rahman (cost pipeline), Logan Wright (telemetry platform)
* Product stakeholders: Aditya Rao (Optimize), Marissa Cole (Console)

h1. What we are validating
h2. Estimator outputs (per suggestion)
Each suggestion (route × model × region × deployment mode) produces:
* Estimated savings:
  * $/day and % reduction (relative to current configuration)
* Estimated performance impact:
  * p50 and p95 latency deltas (ms)
* Risk label:
  * Low / Medium / High (validated separately; see ADR)
* Confidence signals:
  * Telemetry coverage window, variance, stability, and staleness timestamp

h2. Definition of “ground truth” for validation
We use *counterfactual approximations* and *observed before/after deltas* where possible:
1) *Natural experiments* (preferred): routes where configuration changed historically (e.g., caching enabled, batch params adjusted) with sufficient pre/post traffic.
2) *A/B or canary windows* (when available): explicit rollout periods with known change timestamps.
3) *Proxy replay* (fallback): simulate expected improvements using logged distributions and validated microbenchmarks for the same model/hardware class. (Used to bound error; not considered “observed truth”.)

We will label each validation example with a *truth tier* (T1/T2/T3) and report metrics by tier.

h1. Data sources and eligibility
h2. Telemetry sources
* Request aggregates per route/model/region:
  * QPS, concurrency distribution
  * Input/output token counts (p50/p95/p99)
  * Sequence length histograms
  * Latency distributions (p50/p95/p99)
  * Error rate (5xx/429/timeouts)
* Cache metrics (when enabled):
  * Cache hit rate, prefix match rate, eviction rate
  * Cache read/write latency (if instrumented)
* Cost attribution:
  * Per-route cost estimate ($) and/or GPU time proxy, aligned to billing window

h2. Validation window
* Default historical window: last 28 days
* Minimum traffic for inclusion:
  * ≥ 50k requests in the baseline window *and*
  * ≥ 7 distinct days with traffic (to avoid one-off spikes)
* Minimum stability:
  * Baseline p95 latency coefficient of variation (CV) < 0.35 (unless explicitly investigating unstable routes)

h2. Exclusions
* Routes with known incident periods in the baseline or post-change window (unless the change itself is part of incident mitigation)
* Routes with major model version changes confounded with the optimization change (unless we can isolate via pinned model ID)

h1. Sampling strategy
We will build a validation set that is representative and stress-tests edge cases.

h2. Sampling frame
All orgs/projects enrolled in dogfood + selected beta candidates with adequate telemetry maturity.

We stratify by:
* Deployment mode: Hosted / Dedicated / Private (if telemetry is available)
* Model family: Llama-class vs Mistral-class vs other curated open models
* Route type: chat/generate vs embeddings (embeddings generally excluded for batching/caching suggestions beta)
* Region
* Traffic level: high (top 20%), mid (middle 60%), low (bottom 20% but meeting minimum traffic)
* Latency sensitivity: inferred from configured SLO tier (if available) or baseline p95

h2. Sample sizes (targets)
* T1 (natural experiment pre/post): 60 route-model pairs
* T2 (explicit rollout/A-B): 20 route-model pairs
* T3 (proxy replay only): 40 route-model pairs (used for bounding and regression testing)

Total target: 120 validation examples.

h2. Over-sampling known risky areas
We will intentionally include:
* Routes with high variance in input token length
* Routes with spiky concurrency
* Routes with high tail-latency sensitivity (baseline p95 > 2.5× p50)

h1. Methodology
h2. Pre/post analysis (T1/T2)
For each example, define:
* Baseline window: 7 days ending 24h before change
* Post window: 7 days starting 24h after change
(We add the 24h buffer to reduce effects of partial rollouts and cache warm-up.)

Compute observed deltas:
* Cost delta:
  * Δ$/request and Δ$/day (normalized by request volume)
* Latency delta:
  * Δp50, Δp95 (ms)
* Secondary checks:
  * Error rate change (timeouts/5xx), rate limiting events (429)

h2. Matching / confound control
To reduce bias from traffic drift:
* Match baseline and post periods by day-of-week and hour-of-day where possible.
* Reweight by input/output token distributions:
  * If post window has materially different token mix (JS divergence > 0.10), label as “token-shift confounded” and report separately.
* Exclude windows overlapping platform incidents or major capacity changes.

h2. Proxy replay (T3)
Use telemetry distributions (tokens, concurrency) and a hardware/model performance profile to predict:
* Expected throughput change
* Expected latency shift under batching/caching knobs

T3 is used to:
* Detect regressions across estimator versions
* Provide conservative bounds when no pre/post truth exists

h1. Error metrics and reporting
We report metrics at two levels: *per-suggestion* and *portfolio* (top-N suggestions).

h2. Primary error metrics (cost)
For each example i:
* Absolute error (AE): |ŝ_i − s_i|
* Relative error (RE): (ŝ_i − s_i) / max(|s_i|, ε)
  * Use ε = $10/day equivalent to avoid divide-by-near-zero blowups
* Weighted MAPE (wMAPE): Σ|ŝ_i − s_i| / Σ|s_i|
* Bias: mean(ŝ_i − s_i)

Where:
* ŝ_i = estimated savings ($/day)
* s_i = observed savings ($/day) from pre/post normalization

h2. Primary error metrics (latency)
* MAE for Δp50 and Δp95 (ms)
* Tail safety rate:
  * % examples where estimator predicts Δp95 ≤ +X ms but observed Δp95 > +X ms
  * We evaluate X ∈ {25ms, 50ms, 100ms} depending on latency tier

h2. Calibration / confidence evaluation
If estimator emits confidence bands or a confidence score:
* Coverage:
  * % of examples where observed savings falls within predicted [low, high] interval
* Monotonicity:
  * Higher confidence buckets should have lower wMAPE

h2. “Top opportunities” usefulness metric
Since the Console surfaces top opportunities, we evaluate ranking quality:
* Precision@K (K=5,10): fraction of top-K estimated savings suggestions that are also top-K observed savings (within tier)
* NDCG@K for ranking alignment

h2. Reporting slices
We will publish metrics by:
* Suggestion type: batching vs caching
* Truth tier: T1/T2/T3
* Traffic level and variance
* Model family and region

h1. Acceptance thresholds (beta)
These thresholds gate *beta expansion* (not dogfood experimentation).

h2. Cost estimation thresholds
For T1/T2 combined:
* wMAPE (savings $/day):
  * ≤ 35% overall
  * ≤ 45% in each major slice (batching/caching)
* Bias (mean ŝ − s):
  * ≤ +10% of mean observed savings (we prefer slight underestimation; any consistent overestimation must be investigated)
* Overestimation guardrail:
  * P(ŝ > 1.5× s) ≤ 20% for examples with s ≥ $50/day

h2. Latency impact thresholds
For T1/T2 combined:
* MAE(Δp95): ≤ 40ms overall
* Tail safety:
  * For routes labeled Low risk, observed Δp95 > +50ms should occur in ≤ 5% of examples
  * For Medium risk, observed Δp95 > +100ms should occur in ≤ 10% of examples

h2. Confidence and interval quality
If intervals are shown in-product for beta:
* 80% predicted intervals should have ≥ 75% empirical coverage (we accept slight under-coverage in beta but must track)
* Confidence buckets must be monotonic: highest-confidence bucket wMAPE ≤ lowest-confidence bucket wMAPE − 10pp

h2. Console safety requirements (non-metric)
* For any suggestion where telemetry coverage is insufficient (e.g., < 7 days or missing cache signals), the UI must:
  * downgrade confidence,
  * show “insufficient data” caveat, or
  * suppress the suggestion per guardrails.

h1. Beta GA criteria (forward-looking, not required for initial beta)
We will revisit after initial customer feedback, but current targets for GA:
* Cost wMAPE ≤ 25% (T1/T2)
* Overestimation rate P(ŝ > 1.5× s) ≤ 10% for s ≥ $50/day
* MAE(Δp95) ≤ 25ms (T1/T2)
* Clear calibration with published confidence definitions and stable coverage

h1. Execution plan
h2. Deliverables
1) Validation dataset manifest
* List of route/model/org identifiers (pseudonymized), truth tier, change timestamps, eligibility flags.
2) Reproducible evaluation notebook/pipeline
* Produces metric tables and slice dashboards.
3) Findings report
* Summary, key failure modes, recommended estimator adjustments and UI guardrails.

h2. Timeline
* Week 1: Build candidate list and truth-tier labeling; align with telemetry owners on availability.
* Week 2: Run T1/T2 evaluation; compute metrics and slices; identify systemic overestimation.
* Week 3: Patch estimator and re-run; finalize beta acceptance recommendation.

h1. Known failure modes to watch
* Token-shift confounding: post window has different prompt mix.
* Cache warm-up effects: early post window may understate benefits (or overstate if warmed by prior traffic).
* Hidden throttling: concurrency caps or rate-limits change between windows.
* Multi-change confounds: model version, kernel/runtime upgrade, or region capacity change coincident with config change.

Mitigations:
* Buffer windows around changes
* Label and report confounded examples rather than hiding them
* Conservative UI messaging when variance is high

h1. Documentation and customer-facing implications
* If we fail acceptance thresholds:
  * Do not expand beta cohort.
  * Tighten guardrails (suppress low-confidence suggestions) and adjust copy to emphasize uncertainty.
* If we pass thresholds:
  * Proceed with staged beta expansion, but continue to log discrepancies and follow-up with customers reporting mismatches.

h1. Appendix A: Metric definitions (quick reference)
* wMAPE: Σ|error| / Σ|truth|
* MAE: mean(|prediction − truth|)
* Precision@K: overlap of top-K predicted vs top-K truth
* NDCG@K: ranking quality accounting for position

h1. Appendix B: Operational notes
* All analyses must use org/project-scoped data access and comply with telemetry privacy guidelines.
* Any use of customer identifiers in analysis artifacts must be redacted or replaced with stable internal IDs before sharing outside Applied ML/Optimize engineering.

title: Regression triage playbook for Optimize configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization)
preview:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how we investigate quality regressions that appear after an Optimize configuration change (batching policy updates, prefix/KV caching settings, or quantization profile switches). It is written for Applied ML oncall / Evals owners, with clear handoffs to Runtime and SRE.

This playbook is used in two primary situations:
- **A quality gate fails** (smoke or full gate) for a proposed Optimize change.
- **Online canary signals degrade** after a gated change is applied (e.g., increased refusals, task success drop, or customer-reported behavior change).

Non-goals:
- Deep performance tuning (covered by Runtime perf runbooks).
- Model onboarding or prompt set curation (covered by prompt-set ownership docs).

---

## Triage principles
1. **Treat output regressions as real until proven flaky.** Cost optimizations can produce subtle output changes; avoid dismissing failures as “noise” without evidence.
2. **Reproduce on the same model + runtime + hardware SKU.** Quantization regressions are often hardware- and kernel-path dependent.
3. **Prefer smallest reproducible case.** Reduce to a single prompt (or a small handful) that reliably shows the delta.
4. **Capture artifacts early.** Most time is lost when we cannot reconstruct the exact diff/baseline or request context.

---

## Quick decision tree (10–20 minutes)
### Step 0 — Identify what failed
Collect:
- Gate run ID (or rollout/canary ID)
- Change type: batching / caching / quantization
- Model: {model_family, model_version}
- Route/account tier (self-serve vs enterprise)

### Step 1 — Is it an eval harness issue?
Indicators:
- Gate run timed out, infra errors, missing artifacts, GPU starvation
- Flake score elevated; repeated runs disagree

Actions:
- If infra/timeouts: follow **Runbook: Optimize quality gate failures** and re-run with same inputs.
- If inconsistent results: proceed to **Flakiness vs real regression** section.

### Step 2 — Is the regression isolated to a known category?
- **Long-context prompts** (e.g., > 8k tokens) often implicate KV-cache or quant kernels.
- **Tool/function calling or structured output** often implicates decoding differences (temperature/top_p changes via batching policy side effects).
- **Safety/refusal behavior** changes often implicate subtle logit differences with quantization.

### Step 3 — Choose owner and escalation path
- Suspect **batch scheduling / continuous batching / decode** → Runtime (Sofia Ivanova / Mateo Alvarez)
- Suspect **prefix/KV cache correctness** → Runtime caching (Omar El-Sayed / Noah Patel)
- Suspect **quant correctness / kernel path** → Quant (Grace Kim / Arjun Desai)
- Suspect **eval harness / scoring** → Evals (Olivia Grant / Jonas Weber)
- Suspect **production signals / canary pipeline** → SRE + Rollouts (Sean Gallagher / Paula Mendes)

---

## Required artifacts checklist (collect first)
Copy the following into the incident/ticket and attach links as available.

### A) Optimization change metadata
- Optimize recommendation ID (if applicable)
- Config diff (exact)
- Baseline config reference (exact)
- Gate policy used (smoke-only vs smoke+full; thresholds version)
- Apply path: one-click vs auto-apply vs manual change

### B) Gate run / eval metadata
- Gate run ID(s), including retries
- Prompt set IDs and suite versions used
- Sampling parameters: sample size, seed, stratification (if any)
- Scoring version(s): rubric/metric bundle version
- Flake score and rerun/aggregation outcome

### C) Model/runtime/hardware provenance
- Model identifier and commit/hash if applicable
- Tokenizer version (if pinned)
- Runtime build version
- Quantization profile name + parameters (e.g., weight-only int8, kv-cache quant on/off)
- GPU SKU (e.g., A100-80GB, H100, L4) and driver/CUDA version

### D) Input/output artifacts
Minimum:
- 5–20 prompts that demonstrate the regression (or 1 prompt if it’s a hard repro)
- Baseline outputs + optimized outputs
- Decoding parameters used (temperature/top_p/max_tokens/stop)

Preferred for root cause:
- Logprobs (if available) for the first N tokens
- Token-level diff (baseline vs candidate)
- Any error traces (timeouts, OOM, retries)

### E) Online (if canary)
- Canary cohort definition and traffic %
- Time window and region
- Online quality metrics deltas (task success, rubric proxy, refusal rate)
- Latency/cost deltas (to rule out overloaded conditions)

---

## Minimal reproduction setup
The goal is to reproduce the regression **outside of production traffic** while matching runtime behavior.

### 1) Reproduce from a gate run (preferred)
Use the gate run inputs as ground truth.

**Inputs needed:** gate_run_id OR (baseline_config_ref + candidate_config_ref + prompt_set_id)

Procedure:
1. Fetch gate run details and artifacts.
2. Identify the smallest failing suite/prompt subset.
3. Run the harness locally or in a sandbox GPU job using the exact config and model version.

Example (pseudocode CLI; update to current harness tooling):
- `eval-harness gating fetch --gate-run-id <ID> --out ./gate_run_<ID>`
- `eval-harness replay --input ./gate_run_<ID>/repro.json --max-prompts 20 --dump-token-diffs true`

What “good” looks like:
- You can show an identical (or highly similar) score delta.
- You can reproduce the qualitative failure on at least 3 prompts.

### 2) Reproduce from a production request
If the regression is reported by a customer or detected online, capture an anonymized reproduction.

Procedure:
1. Pull a representative request payload (anonymized/redacted) + decoding params.
2. Replay against baseline and candidate configs in the same region and GPU class when possible.
3. Compare outputs and (if enabled) logprobs/token-level diffs.

Example request reproduction template (store as a text artifact, not as nested JSON in this page):
- model: <model>
- prompt/messages: <redacted>
- decoding: temperature=<>, top_p=<>, max_tokens=<>, stop=<>
- baseline_config_ref: <>
- candidate_config_ref: <>
- region + gpu_sku: <>

---

## Narrowing the cause by change type
### A) Batching policy changes
Common failure modes:
- **Non-determinism increase:** larger batch sizes can change ordering/latency; if timeouts or streaming truncation occur, output may change.
- **Decode parameter coupling bug:** some batching paths may incorrectly merge per-request decoding params.
- **Resource contention:** larger microbatches increase memory pressure → OOM fallback paths → degraded quality.

Checks:
1. Confirm decoding params are identical baseline vs candidate per request.
2. Confirm max_tokens/stop are not hit earlier due to timeouts.
3. Compare token counts and finish reasons.
4. If failure is only under high concurrency, reproduce with load (small load test) rather than single-request replay.

Escalate to Runtime when:
- Output differences correlate with concurrency or batch size.
- Any evidence of OOM, fallback kernels, or streaming truncation.

### B) Prefix/KV caching setting changes
Common failure modes:
- **Incorrect cache keying / collisions:** wrong reuse across prompts/routes.
- **Cache invalidation errors:** stale KV cache used after config/model changes.
- **Long-context degradation:** cache policies may change attention behavior indirectly when combined with specific runtime paths.

Checks:
1. Disable cache and confirm regression disappears (A/B run: cache on vs off).
2. Test with long-context prompts and forced cache boundaries.
3. Verify that model version and tokenizer match between cached and uncached runs.
4. Verify prefix caching boundaries align with prompt formatting (chat templates are frequent culprits).

Escalate to Runtime caching when:
- Regression appears only when cache is enabled.
- Failures are prompt-template dependent.

### C) Quantization profile changes
Common failure modes:
- **Kernel-path dependent correctness issues** (GPU SKU specific).
- **Outlier sensitivity**: some prompts become brittle; failures may concentrate in “hard cases.”
- **Safety/refusal shifts** due to logit perturbations.

Checks:
1. Reproduce on the same GPU SKU as the failing run.
2. Compare outputs with logprobs if possible; identify early divergence token.
3. Run a small sweep of prompt lengths (short vs long) to see if divergence correlates with context length.
4. Validate that the quant profile is compatible with the model family and runtime build.

Escalate to Quant owner when:
- Regression is GPU-SKU-specific or only appears under long context.
- Divergence is consistent and starts early in generation.

---

## Flakiness vs real regression
We consider a gate failure “likely flaky” only if we can demonstrate instability across reruns and no consistent qualitative failure.

### Indicators of flakiness
- Re-run outcomes oscillate pass/fail with overlapping confidence intervals.
- The failing prompts vary widely across reruns.
- The flake score is above the current quarantine threshold.

### Procedure
1. Rerun the same suite with fixed seed and fixed sampling.
2. Increase sample size (within budget) to reduce variance.
3. Look for a stable subset of prompts that consistently regress.
4. If none exists and infra is healthy, file a prompt-set flakiness issue and consider quarantine.

Quarantine criteria (guideline; confirm against current policy):
- Suite repeatedly fails reproducibility checks AND causes alert storms.
- Suite exhibits high variance without stable failing prompts.

Actions if quarantined:
- Mark suite as quarantined with a reason code.
- Ensure gates fall back to remaining required coverage.
- Create follow-up task to repair suite (owner + SLA).

---

## Standard “regression packet” (attach to ticket)
When escalating, include a compact packet so Runtime/SRE can act quickly.

Template:
- Summary: <one paragraph>
- Impact: gate blocked apply / canary degraded / customer report
- Change type: batching/caching/quant
- Model + version: <>
- GPU SKU + region: <>
- Baseline config ref: <>
- Candidate config ref: <>
- Gate run ID(s): <>
- Prompt set(s): <>
- Minimal repro prompts: <link or inline list>
- Output comparison: <link to diffs>
- Evidence of determinism: stable across reruns? yes/no
- Logs/traces: <links>
- Initial hypothesis: <1–2 bullets>

---

## Guidance for “fix vs override” decisions
Overrides are allowed only under the exception policy. This section is guidance for triage; always follow the policy for approval and audit requirements.

### Recommend “fix / block apply” when
- Regression reproduces consistently on a small prompt set.
- Regression affects core task success, safety, or structured output correctness.
- Root cause likely correctness (cache collision, quant kernel bug).

### Consider a time-bound override only when
- Evidence strongly supports false positive (flaky suite) OR regression is isolated to a non-critical edge case with documented customer acceptance.
- A rollback/fallback plan exists and is tested.
- The override includes an explicit expiration and a follow-up issue.

---

## Communication and handoffs
- For gate failures affecting multiple teams, open a single tracking ticket and tag:
  - Evals (Applied ML)
  - Runtime owner for the change type
  - SRE if there are pipeline/timeout issues
- Post a short update in the relevant channel with:
  - Gate run ID
  - Model + config diff summary
  - Current status (repro achieved? suspected cause?)
  - Next action + owner

---

## Appendix: Common symptoms and what they usually mean
- **Only fails in EU-West**: likely capacity/timeouts or region-specific GPU mix; involve SRE.
- **Only fails on long-context prompts**: KV cache boundaries or quant kernel path; involve Runtime/Quant.
- **Scores drop but outputs look “fine”**: scoring/rubric mismatch; verify metric bundle version and rubric calibration.
- **Refusal rate spikes**: small logit perturbations from quant; verify safety evaluation subset and compare early-token divergence.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-22: Added explicit artifact checklist for GPU SKU + runtime build; clarified flakiness vs real regression criteria; added regression packet template.
- 2026-02-05: Initial published version aligned to gating MVP (smoke + full gates) and first limited production rollout.

### Step 0 — Identify what failed
Collect:
- Gate run ID (or rollout/canary ID)
- Change type: batching / caching / quantization
- Model: {model_family, model_version}
- Route/account tier (self-serve vs enterprise)

### Step 1 — Is it an eval harness issue?
Indicators:
- Gate run timed out, infra errors, missing artifacts, GPU starvation
- Flake score elevated; repeated runs disagree

Actions:
- If infra/timeouts: follow **Runbook: Optimize quality gate failures** and re-run with same inputs.
- If inconsistent results: proceed to **Flakiness vs real regression** section.

### Step 2 — Is the regression isolated to a known category?
- **Long-context prompts** (e.g., > 8k tokens) often implicate KV-cache or quant kernels.
- **Tool/function calling or structured output** often implicates decoding differences (temperature/top_p changes via batching policy side effects).
- **Safety/refusal behavior** changes often implicate subtle logit differences with quantization.

### Step 3 — Choose owner and escalation path
- Suspect **batch scheduling / continuous batching / decode** → Runtime (Sofia Ivanova / Mateo Alvarez)
- Suspect **prefix/KV cache correctness** → Runtime caching (Omar El-Sayed / Noah Patel)
- Suspect **quant correctness / kernel path** → Quant (Grace Kim / Arjun Desai)
- Suspect **eval harness / scoring** → Evals (Olivia Grant / Jonas Weber)
- Suspect **production signals / canary pipeline** → SRE + Rollouts (Sean Gallagher / Paula Mendes)

---

## Required artifacts checklist (collect first)
Copy the following into the incident/ticket and attach links as available.

### A) Optimization change metadata
- Optimize recommendation ID (if applicable)
- Config diff (exact)
- Baseline config reference (exact)
- Gate policy used (smoke-only vs smoke+full; thresholds version)
- Apply path: one-click vs auto-apply vs manual change

### B) Gate run / eval metadata
- Gate run ID(s), including retries
- Prompt set IDs and suite versions used
- Sampling parameters: sample size, seed, stratification (if any)
- Scoring version(s): rubric/metric bundle version
- Flake score and rerun/aggregation outcome

### C) Model/runtime/hardware provenance
- Model identifier and commit/hash if applicable
- Tokenizer version (if pinned)
- Runtime build version
- Quantization profile name + parameters (e.g., weight-only int8, kv-cache quant on/off)
- GPU SKU (e.g., A100-80GB, H100, L4) and driver/CUDA version

### D) Input/output artifacts
Minimum:
- 5–20 prompts that demonstrate the regression (or 1 prompt if it’s a hard repro)
- Baseline outputs + optimized outputs
- Decoding parameters used (temperature/top_p/max_tokens/stop)

Preferred for root cause:
- Logprobs (if available) for the first N tokens
- Token-level diff (baseline vs candidate)
- Any error traces (timeouts, OOM, retries)

### E) Online (if canary)
- Canary cohort definition and traffic %
- Time window and region
- Online quality metrics deltas (task success, rubric proxy, refusal rate)
- Latency/cost deltas (to rule out overloaded conditions)

---

## Minimal reproduction setup
The goal is to reproduce the regression **outside of production traffic** while matching runtime behavior.

### 1) Reproduce from a gate run (preferred)
Use the gate run inputs as ground truth.

**Inputs needed:** gate_run_id OR (baseline_config_ref + candidate_config_ref + prompt_set_id)

Procedure:
1. Fetch gate run details and artifacts.
2. Identify the smallest failing suite/prompt subset.
3. Run the harness locally or in a sandbox GPU job using the exact config and model version.

Example (pseudocode CLI; update to current harness tooling):
- `eval-harness gating fetch --gate-run-id <ID> --out ./gate_run_<ID>`
- `eval-harness replay --input ./gate_run_<ID>/repro.json --max-prompts 20 --dump-token-diffs true`

What “good” looks like:
- You can show an identical (or highly similar) score delta.
- You can reproduce the qualitative failure on at least 3 prompts.

### 2) Reproduce from a production request
If the regression is reported by a customer or detected online, capture an anonymized reproduction.

Procedure:
1. Pull a representative request payload (anonymized/redacted) + decoding params.
2. Replay against baseline and candidate configs in the same region and GPU class when possible.
3. Compare outputs and (if enabled) logprobs/token-level diffs.

Example request reproduction template (store as a text artifact, not as nested JSON in this page):
- model: <model>
- prompt/messages: <redacted>
- decoding: temperature=<>, top_p=<>, max_tokens=<>, stop=<>
- baseline_config_ref: <>
- candidate_config_ref: <>
- region + gpu_sku: <>

---

## Narrowing the cause by change type
### A) Batching policy changes
Common failure modes:
- **Non-determinism increase:** larger batch sizes can change ordering/latency; if timeouts or streaming truncation occur, output may change.
- **Decode parameter coupling bug:** some batching paths may incorrectly merge per-request decoding params.
- **Resource contention:** larger microbatches increase memory pressure → OOM fallback paths → degraded quality.

Checks:
1. Confirm decoding params are identical baseline vs candidate per request.
2. Confirm max_tokens/stop are not hit earlier due to timeouts.
3. Compare token counts and finish reasons.
4. If failure is only under high concurrency, reproduce with load (small load test) rather than single-request replay.

Escalate to Runtime when:
- Output differences correlate with concurrency or batch size.
- Any evidence of OOM, fallback kernels, or streaming truncation.

### B) Prefix/KV caching setting changes
Common failure modes:
- **Incorrect cache keying / collisions:** wrong reuse across prompts/routes.
- **Cache invalidation errors:** stale KV cache used after config/model changes.
- **Long-context degradation:** cache policies may change attention behavior indirectly when combined with specific runtime paths.

Checks:
1. Disable cache and confirm regression disappears (A/B run: cache on vs off).
2. Test with long-context prompts and forced cache boundaries.
3. Verify that model version and tokenizer match between cached and uncached runs.
4. Verify prefix caching boundaries align with prompt formatting (chat templates are frequent culprits).

Escalate to Runtime caching when:
- Regression appears only when cache is enabled.
- Failures are prompt-template dependent.

### C) Quantization profile changes
Common failure modes:
- **Kernel-path dependent correctness issues** (GPU SKU specific).
- **Outlier sensitivity**: some prompts become brittle; failures may concentrate in “hard cases.”
- **Safety/refusal shifts** due to logit perturbations.

Checks:
1. Reproduce on the same GPU SKU as the failing run.
2. Compare outputs with logprobs if possible; identify early divergence token.
3. Run a small sweep of prompt lengths (short vs long) to see if divergence correlates with context length.
4. Validate that the quant profile is compatible with the model family and runtime build.

Escalate to Quant owner when:
- Regression is GPU-SKU-specific or only appears under long context.
- Divergence is consistent and starts early in generation.

---

## Flakiness vs real regression
We consider a gate failure “likely flaky” only if we can demonstrate instability across reruns and no consistent qualitative failure.

### Indicators of flakiness
- Re-run outcomes oscillate pass/fail with overlapping confidence intervals.
- The failing prompts vary widely across reruns.
- The flake score is above the current quarantine threshold.

### Procedure
1. Rerun the same suite with fixed seed and fixed sampling.
2. Increase sample size (within budget) to reduce variance.
3. Look for a stable subset of prompts that consistently regress.
4. If none exists and infra is healthy, file a prompt-set flakiness issue and consider quarantine.

Quarantine criteria (guideline; confirm against current policy):
- Suite repeatedly fails reproducibility checks AND causes alert storms.
- Suite exhibits high variance without stable failing prompts.

Actions if quarantined:
- Mark suite as quarantined with a reason code.
- Ensure gates fall back to remaining required coverage.
- Create follow-up task to repair suite (owner + SLA).

---

## Standard “regression packet” (attach to ticket)
When escalating, include a compact packet so Runtime/SRE can act quickly.

Template:
- Summary: <one paragraph>
- Impact: gate blocked apply / canary degraded / customer report
- Change type: batching/caching/quant
- Model + version: <>
- GPU SKU + region: <>
- Baseline config ref: <>
- Candidate config ref: <>
- Gate run ID(s): <>
- Prompt set(s): <>
- Minimal repro prompts: <link or inline list>
- Output comparison: <link to diffs>
- Evidence of determinism: stable across reruns? yes/no
- Logs/traces: <links>
- Initial hypothesis: <1–2 bullets>

---

## Guidance for “fix vs override” decisions
Overrides are allowed only under the exception policy. This section is guidance for triage; always follow the policy for approval and audit requirements.

### Recommend “fix / block apply” when
- Regression reproduces consistently on a small prompt set.
- Regression affects core task success, safety, or structured output correctness.
- Root cause likely correctness (cache collision, quant kernel bug).

### Consider a time-bound override only when
- Evidence strongly supports false positive (flaky suite) OR regression is isolated to a non-critical edge case with documented customer acceptance.
- A rollback/fallback plan exists and is tested.
- The override includes an explicit expiration and a follow-up issue.

---

## Communication and handoffs
- For gate failures affecting multiple teams, open a single tracking ticket and tag:
  - Evals (Applied ML)
  - Runtime owner for the change type
  - SRE if there are pipeline/timeout issues
- Post a short update in the relevant channel with:
  - Gate run ID
  - Model + config diff summary
  - Current status (repro achieved? suspected cause?)
  - Next action + owner

---

## Appendix: Common symptoms and what they usually mean
- **Only fails in EU-West**: likely capacity/timeouts or region-specific GPU mix; involve SRE.
- **Only fails on long-context prompts**: KV cache boundaries or quant kernel path; involve Runtime/Quant.
- **Scores drop but outputs look “fine”**: scoring/rubric mismatch; verify metric bundle version and rubric calibration.
- **Refusal rate spikes**: small logit perturbations from quant; verify safety evaluation subset and compare early-token divergence.

---

## Change log
- 2026-02-22: Added explicit artifact checklist for GPU SKU + runtime build; clarified flakiness vs real regression criteria; added regression packet template.
- 2026-02-05: Initial published version aligned to gating MVP (smoke + full gates) and first limited production rollout.

title: Cost Model Validation Harness (v1) — Golden Datasets, Replay, and Acceptance Criteria
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the validation harness for the Standard Cost Model (SCM) v1. The harness is the shared mechanism to:
- Verify correctness and determinism of per-request cost projections.
- Prevent regressions as schemas/runtime telemetry evolve.
- Quantify and monitor drift between predicted cost and observed spend.
- Provide repeatable evidence for Console/Optimize adoption (and Finance alignment).

The harness is explicitly *not* a billing reconciliation system of record. It is a correctness/regression suite for the cost model spec + implementation.

## Scope
### In scope (v1)
- Golden fixtures for SCM inputs (request + deployment context + runtime/telemetry-derived primitives) and expected SCM outputs.
- Replay runner that executes the Cost Model API/service against fixtures in CI and nightly.
- Acceptance thresholds per output field and per aggregate view (prefill/decode split, cache attribution, overhead allocation).
- Integration points with:
  - Serving runtime perf suite (latency/throughput counters used as SCM inputs)
  - Drift monitoring job (predicted vs observed)

### Out of scope (v1)
- Full invoice reconciliation and RevRec.
- “Exhaustive” model catalog coverage. v1 uses a curated coverage set.
- Customer-facing quality metrics for generation output; only cost-model correctness is validated.

## Definitions
- **Fixture**: A single test case capturing SCM inputs and expected outputs (with tolerances).
- **Golden dataset**: A versioned collection of fixtures representing realistic workload slices.
- **Replay**: Running the SCM compute against fixtures using a pinned schema version and pinned pricing inputs.
- **Observed cost**: Finance-approved spend attribution for a reporting window (e.g., GPU-hours + overhead pools) transformed into comparable primitives.
- **Drift**: Relative error between predicted and observed cost for the same workload slice and window.

## Harness architecture (conceptual)
1) *Fixture builder* produces SCM input payloads from:
   - Sanitized telemetry extracts (token accounting, cache stats, batching stats)
   - Model catalog metadata (model id, quant profile id)
   - Deployment context (hosted/dedicated/private; region; hardware SKU)
2) *Replay runner* calls Cost Model API (preferred) or library entrypoint (fallback) with:
   - schema_version = pinned (e.g., "cost-model/v1")
   - pricing_snapshot_id = pinned (e.g., "2026-02-01")
3) *Comparator* checks outputs against expected values with field-level tolerances.
4) *Reporter* emits:
   - CI pass/fail signal with diffs
   - trend metrics for nightly runs
   - coverage report by scenario

## Golden datasets
Golden datasets are designed to cover the most common and most error-prone dimensions.

### Dataset catalog (v1)
The following datasets must exist for v1 launch.

| Dataset ID | Primary purpose | Size target | Update cadence | Owner |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| golden-hosted-chat-baseline | Basic hosted chat requests, no cache | 200 fixtures | Quarterly + on major schema changes | Telemetry & Analytics |
| golden-hosted-streaming | Streaming + partial completions + retries | 100 fixtures | Quarterly | Platform Control Plane |
| golden-dedicated-batch | Batch efficiency + queueing attribution | 150 fixtures | Quarterly | Runtime Scheduling |
| golden-kv-cache-scenarios | Prefix/KV cache hit/miss attribution | 150 fixtures | Monthly until stable | Runtime Scheduling |
| golden-quant-profiles | Quant profile deltas & constraints | 120 fixtures | On new quant profiles | Applied ML |
| golden-overhead-allocation | Overhead allocation behavior per policy | 60 fixtures | On Finance policy update | FP&A |
| golden-cardinality-bounds | Stress tests for bounding/pagination behavior | 40 fixtures | Quarterly | Platform Control Plane |

### Fixture scenario dimensions
Each dataset should intentionally cover:
- Deployment mode: hosted / dedicated / private
- Region: at least 2 regions with different SKU/pricing (e.g., us-east, eu-west)
- Hardware SKU: at least 2 GPU SKUs per deployment mode used in production
- Request shape: prompt length distribution, max_tokens, stop/early_stop
- Cache: kv_cache_hit_rate buckets (0%, ~50%, ~90%), cache warm/cold
- Batching: batch_size buckets (1, 4, 16+) and batch efficiency variations
- Quantization: fp16/bf16 baseline + at least one quant profile id

### Data hygiene and privacy
- Fixtures must not include customer prompts or PII.
- Prompts in fixtures must be synthetic or templated (e.g., "Summarize the following: <DOC_001>").
- Any real telemetry used to derive distributions must be aggregated/sanitized before fixture construction.

## Replay methodology
### Execution modes
- **CI (PR gating)**:
  - Runs a small, fast subset: ~50–100 fixtures total across key datasets.
  - Enforces determinism and strict correctness checks.
  - Runtime budget: 3 minutes wall-clock.
- **Nightly (extended)**:
  - Runs full datasets.
  - Computes drift metrics and trendlines; posts summary to #eng-platform.
  - Runtime budget: 30–45 minutes.

### Pinning and reproducibility
To make comparisons stable, the runner must pin:
- schema_version (e.g., "cost-model/v1")
- pricing_snapshot_id (e.g., "2026-02-01")
- model_catalog_snapshot (e.g., "catalog-2026-02-10")
- runtime-metrics-contract version (for derived fields)

Any change to a pinned artifact requires an explicit update PR to the harness with:
- rationale
- expected diff summary
- reviewer sign-off from owning team

### Calling surface
Preferred: validate via Cost Model API endpoints used by Console/Optimize (to catch serialization/auth/bounds behavior). Fallback: library compute for unit-level invariants.

The harness supports two call patterns:
1) Per-request estimate endpoint (single fixture → estimate)
2) Aggregated breakdown endpoint (workload summary → breakdown)
3) What-if endpoint (baseline + scenario change → projected delta)

## Acceptance criteria
Acceptance thresholds are designed to:
- Be tight where determinism is expected (pure arithmetic on pinned inputs).
- Be tolerant where empirical models/heuristics exist (batch efficiency, cache warm-rate, uncertainty intervals).

### Field-level tolerances (v1)
| Output field class | Examples | Tolerance | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Exact deterministic scalars | token counts, stage attribution totals | 0 (exact match) | Must match exactly with pinned contracts |
| Monetary totals (per-request) | total_cost_usd | ±0.5% or ±$0.0001 (whichever larger) | Accounts for rounding; should normally be tighter |
| Breakdown components | prefill_cost, decode_cost, cache_savings | ±1.0% of component | Component drift must not cancel out to hide errors |
| Overhead allocation | overhead_allocated_usd | Policy-dependent; ±2.0% | Must follow Finance policy doc; review on policy change |
| What-if deltas | delta_total_cost_usd | ±3.0% | Wider due to modeled effects; must also satisfy directionality checks |
| Confidence/uncertainty | ci_low/ci_high | Interval must contain observed value ≥ 90% in nightly backtests | Evaluated on dataset-level, not per fixture |

### Invariant checks (must always pass)
- Non-negativity: all costs >= 0.
- Conservation: total_cost ≈ sum(breakdown components) within rounding tolerance.
- Stage consistency: prefill_tokens + decode_tokens = total_billed_tokens (for schemas that require it).
- Cache accounting: cache_savings cannot exceed baseline prefill+decode cost for identical request.
- Bounded outputs: responses must not exceed documented max list lengths / bucket counts.

### Drift acceptance (nightly/backtest)
Drift is evaluated at the dataset aggregate level (not per-request):
- P50 absolute percentage error (APE) <= 5%
- P95 APE <= 12%
- No single dataset aggregate APE > 15% for 2 consecutive nights

When drift breaches thresholds, the harness must:
- open an issue tagged "cost-model-drift"
- attach the dataset aggregate diff and top contributing scenarios (by cost)

## How perf/eval suites validate SCM projections
### Runtime perf suite integration
The serving runtime perf suite provides the authoritative source for:
- prefill vs decode token attribution
- batch efficiency metrics and queue time counters
- kv cache hit/miss counters
- quantization execution path identifiers

Validation approach:
1) On a fixed set of perf workloads (prompt/response sizes), record runtime metrics.
2) Convert metrics into SCM inputs using the published runtime metrics contract.
3) Ensure SCM output breakdown behaves monotonically with respect to:
   - Increasing batch efficiency should not increase per-token compute cost (holding other vars constant).
   - Increasing kv-cache hit rate should not increase prefill compute cost.
   - Quant profile switching should reflect expected throughput/memory deltas per catalog metadata.

### Optimize “what-if” regression checks
For each supported what-if knob (v1):
- batching policy change
- caching enablement
- quantization profile swap

The harness includes paired fixtures:
- baseline payload
- what-if payload

Required checks:
- Directionality: if a what-if claims cost savings, delta_total_cost_usd must be <= 0.
- Guardrails: projected savings must be bounded by physically plausible limits (e.g., cannot exceed removing all decode compute).
- Confidence sanity: intervals should widen when input uncertainty increases (e.g., unknown warm-rate).

## Tooling and implementation plan
### Repo layout (proposed)
- /cost_model/validation_harness/
  - datasets/
  - fixtures/
  - runner/
  - comparator/
  - reports/

### CLI runner (proposed)
Commands:
- cost-model-validate run --dataset golden-hosted-chat-baseline --env staging
- cost-model-validate run --dataset ci-smoke --env local --use-library
- cost-model-validate report --last-nightly

### CI integration
- PRs touching any of:
  - schema package
  - cost-model service compute
  - telemetry input contracts
  - pricing snapshot logic
  - model catalog quant metadata

…must trigger the CI subset validation.

### Updating goldens
Golden updates must be explicit and reviewed.
Process:
1) Run runner with --update-goldens in a dedicated branch.
2) Generate diff summary (top 20 fixtures by cost change, and reason tags).
3) Obtain approvals:
   - Owning team for affected dataset
   - Finance reviewer if overhead allocation outputs change
4) Merge with a changelog entry referencing the PR/ADR.

## Failure triage playbook (quick reference)
When CI fails:
1) Determine failure type:
   - Schema mismatch/serialization
   - Determinism (non-reproducible output)
   - Numeric regression (cost changed)
   - Bounds/pagination violation
2) Identify affected datasets and scenario dimensions.
3) If regression is expected:
   - update goldens with justification and reviewer approvals
4) If unexpected:
   - bisect by recent changes to telemetry contracts, pricing snapshot, or model catalog
   - validate on staging with same pinned artifacts

## Open questions (tracked for v1 closeout)
- Final tolerance for overhead allocation under partial telemetry (telemetry lag scenarios).
- Whether to require strict matching for quantization deltas per profile, or allow empirical calibration to override catalog metadata.
- How to represent cache warm-rate assumptions in fixtures without reintroducing high-cardinality inputs.

## Owners and contacts
- Harness owner: Leila Farouk (E2E/automation QA)
- Drift/backtesting: Mei Lin (Analytics pipelines), Logan Wright (Telemetry)
- What-if methodology: Jonas Weber (Applied ML)
- Runtime metrics contract: Sofia Ivanova, Omar El-Sayed
- Service/API issues: Ethan Park

## Appendix A: Example fixture (human-readable)
Each fixture records a minimal SCM input payload, plus expected outputs and tolerances. The canonical serialized form lives alongside the dataset.

Example (conceptual):
- Input: hosted, us-east, sku=H100-80GB, model=rw-llama-70b, quant=bf16
- Request: prompt_tokens=1200, decode_tokens=250, streaming=true
- Runtime: avg_batch_size=8, batch_efficiency=0.72, kv_cache_hit_rate=0.55
- Expected:
  - total_cost_usd=… (±0.5%)
  - breakdown: prefill/decode/cache_savings/overhead (component tolerances)

Appendix B: Change log
- 2026-02-22: Added drift thresholds and what-if directionality checks; aligned overhead tolerance to latest Finance guidance.
- 2026-02-10: Expanded dataset catalog to include cardinality bounds and streaming scenarios.
- 2026-01-23: Initial draft.

| Confidence/uncertainty | ci_low/ci_high | Interval must contain observed value ≥ 90% in nightly backtests | Evaluated on dataset-level, not per fixture |

### Invariant checks (must always pass)
- Non-negativity: all costs >= 0.
- Conservation: total_cost ≈ sum(breakdown components) within rounding tolerance.
- Stage consistency: prefill_tokens + decode_tokens = total_billed_tokens (for schemas that require it).
- Cache accounting: cache_savings cannot exceed baseline prefill+decode cost for identical request.
- Bounded outputs: responses must not exceed documented max list lengths / bucket counts.

### Drift acceptance (nightly/backtest)
Drift is evaluated at the dataset aggregate level (not per-request):
- P50 absolute percentage error (APE) <= 5%
- P95 APE <= 12%
- No single dataset aggregate APE > 15% for 2 consecutive nights

When drift breaches thresholds, the harness must:
- open an issue tagged "cost-model-drift"
- attach the dataset aggregate diff and top contributing scenarios (by cost)

## How perf/eval suites validate SCM projections
### Runtime perf suite integration
The serving runtime perf suite provides the authoritative source for:
- prefill vs decode token attribution
- batch efficiency metrics and queue time counters
- kv cache hit/miss counters
- quantization execution path identifiers

Validation approach:
1) On a fixed set of perf workloads (prompt/response sizes), record runtime metrics.
2) Convert metrics into SCM inputs using the published runtime metrics contract.
3) Ensure SCM output breakdown behaves monotonically with respect to:
   - Increasing batch efficiency should not increase per-token compute cost (holding other vars constant).
   - Increasing kv-cache hit rate should not increase prefill compute cost.
   - Quant profile switching should reflect expected throughput/memory deltas per catalog metadata.

### Optimize “what-if” regression checks
For each supported what-if knob (v1):
- batching policy change
- caching enablement
- quantization profile swap

The harness includes paired fixtures:
- baseline payload
- what-if payload

Required checks:
- Directionality: if a what-if claims cost savings, delta_total_cost_usd must be <= 0.
- Guardrails: projected savings must be bounded by physically plausible limits (e.g., cannot exceed removing all decode compute).
- Confidence sanity: intervals should widen when input uncertainty increases (e.g., unknown warm-rate).

## Tooling and implementation plan
### Repo layout (proposed)
- /cost_model/validation_harness/
  - datasets/
  - fixtures/
  - runner/
  - comparator/
  - reports/

### CLI runner (proposed)
Commands:
- cost-model-validate run --dataset golden-hosted-chat-baseline --env staging
- cost-model-validate run --dataset ci-smoke --env local --use-library
- cost-model-validate report --last-nightly

### CI integration
- PRs touching any of:
  - schema package
  - cost-model service compute
  - telemetry input contracts
  - pricing snapshot logic
  - model catalog quant metadata

…must trigger the CI subset validation.

### Updating goldens
Golden updates must be explicit and reviewed.
Process:
1) Run runner with --update-goldens in a dedicated branch.
2) Generate diff summary (top 20 fixtures by cost change, and reason tags).
3) Obtain approvals:
   - Owning team for affected dataset
   - Finance reviewer if overhead allocation outputs change
4) Merge with a changelog entry referencing the PR/ADR.

## Failure triage playbook (quick reference)
When CI fails:
1) Determine failure type:
   - Schema mismatch/serialization
   - Determinism (non-reproducible output)
   - Numeric regression (cost changed)
   - Bounds/pagination violation
2) Identify affected datasets and scenario dimensions.
3) If regression is expected:
   - update goldens with justification and reviewer approvals
4) If unexpected:
   - bisect by recent changes to telemetry contracts, pricing snapshot, or model catalog
   - validate on staging with same pinned artifacts

## Open questions (tracked for v1 closeout)
- Final tolerance for overhead allocation under partial telemetry (telemetry lag scenarios).
- Whether to require strict matching for quantization deltas per profile, or allow empirical calibration to override catalog metadata.
- How to represent cache warm-rate assumptions in fixtures without reintroducing high-cardinality inputs.

## Owners and contacts
- Harness owner: Leila Farouk (E2E/automation QA)
- Drift/backtesting: Mei Lin (Analytics pipelines), Logan Wright (Telemetry)
- What-if methodology: Jonas Weber (Applied ML)
- Runtime metrics contract: Sofia Ivanova, Omar El-Sayed
- Service/API issues: Ethan Park

## Appendix A: Example fixture (human-readable)
Each fixture records a minimal SCM input payload, plus expected outputs and tolerances. The canonical serialized form lives alongside the dataset.

Example (conceptual):
- Input: hosted, us-east, sku=H100-80GB, model=rw-llama-70b, quant=bf16
- Request: prompt_tokens=1200, decode_tokens=250, streaming=true
- Runtime: avg_batch_size=8, batch_efficiency=0.72, kv_cache_hit_rate=0.55
- Expected:
  - total_cost_usd=… (±0.5%)
  - breakdown: prefill/decode/cache_savings/overhead (component tolerances)

Appendix B: Change log
- 2026-02-22: Added drift thresholds and what-if directionality checks; aligned overhead tolerance to latest Finance guidance.
- 2026-02-10: Expanded dataset catalog to include cardinality bounds and streaming scenarios.
- 2026-01-23: Initial draft.

title: Shadow Orchestrator for Cross-Modal Stress Testing and Shadow Drift Detection
preview:
Overview
========
This playbook describes the Shadow Orchestrator: an orchestrated, repeatable pipeline for running cross-modal (text + image + long-context) shadow tests against production traffic to detect regressions, quantization sensitivity, and early drift. The goal is to exercise the full serving stack and evaluation harness without impacting live responses.

Why this exists
---------------
- Catch subtle cross-modal regressions that only appear under production request composition (multimodal prompts, long context + tool calls).
- Measure behavior differences introduced by model promotions, quantization profiles, or runtime kernel changes.
- Provide a fast, audit-friendly path for risk-aware promotion decisions.

Scope
-----
- Applies to hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where shadowing of selective traffic is supported.
- Supports: text-only, text+image, and long-context (>4k tokens) shadow runs.
- Not intended for full load performance benchmarking (use dedicated perf suite).

Key components
--------------
1) Orchestrator controller: schedules shadow runs, enforces traffic fraction, and records run metadata.
2) Probe generator: synthesizes cross-modal probes from prompt catalog and production anchors.
3) Shadow sequencer: replays requests into shadow model variants while preserving KV cache warmup policy.
4) Telemetry collector: aggregates semantic metrics, token deltas, latency/throughput, and KV-cache hit rates.
5) Comparator & alerting: computes divergence metrics and raises triage alerts when thresholds are exceeded.

Run definitions and config (example)
----------------------------------
- run_type: cross_modal_shadow
- traffic_fraction: 0.5%  # percent of qualifying requests to fork to shadow path
- probe_mix: [ real-anchors: 70%, synthetic-probes: 30% ]
- variants: [baseline: "v3.1-quant32", candidate: "v3.2-q4f", fallback: "v3.1-quant32"]
- kv_cache_policy: warmup=2 requests, preserve_prefix=true
- telemetry: semantic_similarity=cosine-sbert, token_perturb=edit-distance-tokenized

YAML example for orchestrator (use with CLI)
--------------------------------------------
run_id: crossmodal-2026-02-20-01
selector:
  sources: ["web-api", "mobile-sdk"]
  modalities: ["text", "image", "long-context"]
  min_tokens: 20
  max_tokens: 8192
probes:
  anchor_sampling: {window_hours: 72, sample_rate: 0.001}
  synthetic_rules: ["image-caption-variants", "noisy-instruction-perturbs"]
variants:
  baseline: "models/redwood-v3.1-quant32"
  candidate: "models/redwood-v3.2-q4f"
  fallback: "models/redwood-v3.1-quant32"
schedule:
  parallelism: 8
  batch_size: 16
  max_runtime_minutes: 180
observability:
  metrics: ["semantic_divergence", "token_perturb_rate", "latency_p99_delta", "kv_cache_hit_frac"]
  report_interval_seconds: 60

CLI examples
------------
- Start run: rwdctl shadow start --config ./crossmodal-run.yaml
- Status: rwdctl shadow status run_id=crossmodal-2026-02-20-01
- Snapshot results: rwdctl shadow export --run crossmodal-2026-02-20-01 --format jsonl

Telemetry and scoring (what we measure)
-------------------------------------
- Semantic divergence (S): cosine similarity delta between baseline and candidate embeddings; aggregated per-slice.
- Token perturbation rate (T): percent of responses with edit-distance token change > 5%.
- Latency P99 delta (L): relative increase in p99 latency for candidate vs baseline.
- KV-cache instability (K): fraction of requests where key-cache leads to different generation path (measured via prefix prefix-match diagnostics).

Alerting thresholds (initial)
---------------------------
- S_mean_delta > 0.08 for any high-importance slice => triage alert.
- T_rate > 0.03 on user-facing prompt family => abort promotion.
- L_p99_increase > 0.15 (15%) across global traffic => performance incident.
- K_instability > 0.05 for multimodal long-context => investigate quantization fallback.

Success criteria for a safe candidate run
---------------------------------------
- No alerting thresholds exceeded on top-50 production slices.
- Semantic divergence concentrated in low-impact slices only (<= 5% of overall traffic).
- Regression precision: when failing probes are re-run with deterministic seeds, true positive regression rate > 60% (reduces noisy alerts).

Procedure: Running a cross-modal shadow run (step-by-step)
--------------------------------------------------------
1) Author a run-config YAML and attach the appropriate probe catalog (see Probe Catalog section below).
2) Tag the candidate model artifact in model catalog and ensure runtime kernels/quant profile are declared.
3) Execute: rwdctl shadow start --config <yaml>. Note owner and run_id.
4) Monitor the run dashboard: focus on semantic_divergence heatmap, token_perturb-slice table, and latency deltas.
5) If any threshold is breached, follow Triage flow (next section).
6) After run completes, publish the run report to the promotion checklist and attach exported JSONL of mismatched pairs.

Triage flow (if alerts triggered)
--------------------------------
1) Create a triage ticket in the eval-triage project and link the run_id and top-5 failing probe examples.
2) Reproduce locally with deterministic seeds: rwdctl shadow replay --run <run_id> --seed 42 --limit 50.
3) Classify failure: semantic-quality | hallucination | formatting | latency | kv-cache-path.
4) If quality regression is confirmed: assign to model-onboarding team for rollback or candidate rework.
5) If instability due to quantization: escalate to quant-profiles owner with artifact + kernel logs.
6) Tag customer-impacting examples and notify customer-success if applicable.

Probe catalog guidance
---------------------
- Prefer a mix: 70% production anchors (anonymized) + 30% synthetic stress probes.
- Include multimodal transforms: rotated/cropped images, compressed images, OCR-noise variants.
- For long-context probes, attach explicit KV-cache priming sequences (3 warmup prompts).

Data handling and privacy
------------------------
- All shadowed request payloads must be anonymized prior to being stored in the eval-mesh: PII redaction, token hashing for user identifiers, and image blurring for sensitive regions.
- Retention: shadow run raw exports retained for 30 days by default; longer retention requires compliance approval.
- When operating in Private deployments, ensure shadow export endpoints route to customer-owned storage where configured.

Integration and dependencies
---------------------------
- Serving: must support forked request path with independent model routing and consistent headers.
- Eval-mesh: probe delivery, result collection, and reproducible seeds.
- Telemetry pipeline: ingest at 1m granularity and store semantic embeddings in vector-store for post-hoc analysis.
- Model catalog: candidate/baseline artifact tags and quant-profile metadata must be present.

Roles & responsibilities
------------------------
| Role | Responsibility | Owner |
|------|----------------|-------|
| Run Owner | Create running config, approve probe set | ML Eng (on-call) |
| Probe Curator | Maintain multimodal probe catalog, labels | Applied ML |
| Telemetry Owner | Validate metric correctness and storage | Observability team |
| Quant Owner | Diagnose and fix quant failures | Research/Applied ML |
| Customer-facing comms | Coordinate customer notifications | Customer Success |

Recovery and rollback patterns
------------------------------
- Fast rollback: disable shadow forks for the candidate and re-promote baseline (rwdctl model promote --model-id <baseline>).
- Partial rollback: restrict candidate to internal-region or dedicated pool; run micro-canary before reattempting promotion.
- Fallback routing: orchestrator can automatically route new requests to 'fallback' variant when token_perturb_rate > 0.05 for a sliding 5m window.

Synthesis: common failure modes and mitigations
----------------------------------------------
- Multimodal alignment drift: increase probe coverage for image perturbations and re-evaluate model fusion layer.
- Quantization hallucination spikes: revert to higher-precision quant profile for affected slices and re-run shadow.
- KV cache divergence on long-context: increase warmup count and ensure cache-key normalization across runtime kernels.

Appendix: useful queries and dashboards
-------------------------------------
- SQL snippet to list top failing slices:
  SELECT slice, count(*) as failures, avg(semantic_divergence) as mean_div 
  FROM shadow_runs.results
  WHERE run_id = 'crossmodal-2026-02-20-01'
  GROUP BY slice
  ORDER BY failures DESC
  LIMIT 50;

- Dashboard: /dashboards/shadow-orchestrator/crossmodal-summary (contains heatmaps, time-series for divergence, token perturb histograms).

Change log
----------
- 2026-01-15: Draft created by Maya Chen.
- 2026-02-20: Added YAML example, clarified KV-cache policy, tightened initial thresholds and published (reviewers Luis, Priya, Samir).

Related artifacts and links
-------------------------
- Probe catalog: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/prompt-sets-and-benchmarks/crossmodal-probe-catalog-2026 (recommended anchor set).
- Model catalog docs: /confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/model-onboarding/production-model-certification-checklist-2026.

Contact
-------
For questions or to request a run: open an internal ticket in the #eval-orchestration Slack channel or contact Maya Chen (maya.chen@redwood.ai).
